This experiment tests whether a learning-to-rank model that incorporates prior alert sequences and incident response context can rank true high-severity incidents higher in the analyst queue than a pointwise classifier trained only on alert metadata. Success is measured by query-averaged AUROC on held-out alerts: a statistically significant improvement would justify the operational deployment of sequence-aware ranking in the SOC workflow.

## 1. Data Strategy

Data is sourced from 12 months of production SIEM/EDR alert logs from the enterprise. Each record includes a timestamp, rule ID, host and user identifiers, initial severity score, enrichment metadata (asset criticality, user role), and analyst disposition (escalated, benign, linked to incident). Approximate volume: 200k–500k alerts, with 2–5% incident-related ground truth.

Labels are defined as follows: an alert is labeled positive (incident-related) if SOC analysts linked it to an incident ticket within 72 hours of generation. Alerts with no disposition after 72 hours are labeled negative. This 72-hour window prevents future label information from leaking into the model at inference time and reflects realistic SOC uncertainty.

A critical data quality issue is partial observation: not all alerts receive a disposition, and labels arrive asynchronously. We address this by treating absence of a disposition as a negative label (conservative choice, slightly biases recall downward but is consistent and unbiased w.r.t. both models).

## 2. Split Strategy

Data is partitioned at the ranking-query level rather than by contiguous month blocks: day/queue queries are assigned to training, validation, and test in an approximately 80/10/10 proportion, with assignment stratified by incident prevalence band, analyst queue, and month-of-year so each split contains comparable mixtures of sparse-incident and busy-queue days. This helps ensure that hyperparameter selection is not overly influenced by a single atypical validation period and that all three splits reflect the full range of operational queue conditions observed over the year.

Within each split, ranking queries are constructed by calendar day and analyst queue: each query is the set of all alerts routed to a single analyst on a single day. This preserves operational context and reflects how alerts are presented to the SOC team.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Feature set (identical for both models, except sequence context):**

*Alert metadata:* rule ID (one-hot encoded), initial severity score (normalized to [0,1]), host ID, user ID.

*Enrichment features:* asset criticality (from CMDB), user role risk score, threat intelligence threat level.

*Sequence context (learning-to-rank model only):* prior alert count from same host (7-day window), prior alert count from same user (7-day window), seconds since most recent prior alert from same host, count of prior alerts on the same rule (24-hour window), binary flag for prior escalation from same host (30-day window).

**Preprocessing fit point:** All transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit on the training split only. The same fitted transformers are applied to validation and test splits without refitting. This prevents data leakage and keeps model inputs consistent across evaluation stages.

Sequence features are computed using only alerts that strictly precede the current alert in time. No future information is encoded in the feature set for any alert.

## 4. Model and Baseline

**Learning-to-rank model:** LambdaMART (gradient boosting on pairwise preferences). The model is trained to maximize ranking quality on training queries (day-queue alert sets). It receives feature vectors for pairs of alerts within a query and learns a ranking function that places incident-related alerts above benign ones.

**Baseline:** Pointwise logistic regression trained on alert metadata and enrichment features *only* (no sequence context). The baseline predicts the probability that an alert is incident-related; these probabilities are used as ranking scores within queries. 

The baseline is fair because: (1) it receives identical preprocessing and feature engineering as the learning-to-rank model (minus sequence context); (2) it is hyperparameter-tuned on the same query-level selection metric as the learning-to-rank model, rather than on a standalone classification objective; (3) it is evaluated under identical query and metric conditions. This isolates the hypothesis: any ranking improvement is due to sequence context and pairwise learning, not to superior feature access or tuning.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Query-averaged AUROC, computed within each day/queue query and then averaged over all test queries. This metric measures how consistently the model separates incident-related from benign alerts across heterogeneous analyst queues without locking the analysis to a single queue-depth cutoff that may vary with workload. Because queue sizes and incident counts differ substantially by analyst and day, the within-query AUROC formulation also makes stratified analysis cleaner when comparing sparse and dense queues.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall@20: proportion of all test-set incidents appearing in top 20 of their query.
- Mean reciprocal rank (MRR): average rank of the first incident in each query (time-to-first-incident).
- Precision@20: proportion of top-20 alerts that are incidents.
- nDCG@20: a cutoff-sensitive ranking metric included as a supplementary check on top-of-queue behavior.

## 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation split only. Both models are trained on the training split, evaluated on the validation split using query-averaged AUROC, and the configuration with the highest validation AUROC is selected. The test set is not touched during tuning.

After tuning, both models are retrained on the combined training + validation splits with the tuned hyperparameters and evaluated on the held-out test split exactly once. Results include 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap over test queries) and significance tests (permutation test or paired t-test).

## 7. Confound Controls

**Queue heterogeneity:** Stratifying the query-level partition by analyst queue, incident prevalence band, and month-of-year keeps the train/validation/test comparison from being dominated by unusually sparse or unusually heavy queue conditions.

**Feature access parity:** Both models receive identical metadata and enrichment features. The baseline is not starved; the learning-to-rank model's advantage is solely from sequence context and pairwise learning.

**Ablation check:** A secondary analysis trains the learning-to-rank model *without* sequence features. If performance matches the baseline, the gain is confirmed to be from sequence context, not from other modeling differences.

**Label bias:** All alerts without disposition are treated as benign (conservative, consistent, unbiased across models).

**Population shift:** After training, incident rate distribution across test queries is compared to training/validation. If shift is detected, results are stratified by incident rate decile.

## 8. Scope and Limitations

This experiment tests whether sequence context and pairwise learning improve ranking *within this organization's alert stream*. It does not establish that this approach generalizes to other SOCs, alert types, or rule sets. It measures ranking quality, not end-to-end SOC outcomes (e.g., mean time to containment) — those require operational deployment and longer-term measurement.

hypothesis: A transformer-based ranker that uses both query text and product title/attribute embeddings will improve top-10 search relevance over a BM25-plus-handcrafted-features baseline for e-commerce product search, as measured by offline nDCG and online click-through rate.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search and retrieval for a large retail catalog with millions of products and logged search sessions
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Ranker for E-Commerce Search

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of production search logs from the e-commerce marketplace. Raw data consists of query impressions (query text, timestamp, session ID, device), product impressions in rank positions 1–10+, and implicit feedback (clicks, add-to-cart, purchase events). In parallel, a held-out human judgment set of approximately 5,000–10,000 query-product pairs is collected through independent expert labeling on a 0–4 graded relevance scale (not relevant through perfect match). This human set is kept separate from the training pipeline to provide unbiased ground truth.

Implicit click labels are corrected for position bias using inverse propensity weighting (IPW). Position 1 naturally accumulates more clicks than position 10 regardless of true relevance; IPW reweights each click by 1/P(exposure), where P(exposure) is the empirical probability the pair appeared in that position during training. This produces an unbiased signal for training. Unlabeled pairs (no clicks) are treated as missing data, not as negative examples.

### 2. Train/Validation/Test Split Strategy

Data is split temporally to prevent leakage and evaluate under realistic distribution shift. The split is:
- **Training:** Months 1–12 (~50% of search volume, establishing baseline catalog and query patterns)
- **Validation:** Months 13–15 (~25% of volume, introduced catalog change and seasonal variation)
- **Test:** Months 16–18 (~25% of volume, forward-looking evaluation)

The human judgment set is allocated 60%/40% to validation/test reference sets respectively, preserving statistical independence. Queries and products are not shuffled; instead, the split respects chronological order to capture real-world distribution shifts (new products, seasonal demand, query pattern drift). This temporal approach ensures the model is tested on unseen time periods and new data, not on a random sample from the same distribution as training.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing steps are fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. Query and product text are tokenized using a learned vocabulary (minimum frequency threshold based on training data). For the transformer ranker, dual-encoder transformers (either BERT or a domain-fine-tuned variant) are initialized with public weights and fine-tuned only on training data. Product representations concatenate title and key attributes (brand, category, salient structured attributes). Query-product interactions are captured through element-wise operations, distance metrics, and cosine similarity.

The baseline (BM25 + handcrafted features) computes BM25 statistics from training data and derives feature distributions (e.g., price percentiles by category) that are frozen before validation. Both models receive the same auxiliary signals: log-normalized product popularity, log-scaled price, brand affinity, and catalog freshness (days since product addition).

### 4. Model Training and Baseline

The transformer model is a listwise neural ranker: dual-encoder transformers produce query and product embeddings, combined through an interaction layer and feed-forward scoring head (2–3 hidden layers) to produce a relevance score. Training uses a listwise loss (LambdaLoss, ListNet, or Approx-NDCG) applied to query-level batches, with IPW position weighting. Hyperparameters (embedding dimension, encoder depth, learning rate, regularization) are tuned on the validation set.

The baseline is BM25 + a gradient boosted tree (LightGBM) trained on BM25 scores and handcrafted features using the same training data, same listwise loss, and same position bias weighting. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort (grid search or Bayesian optimization) on the validation set. This ensures any difference in test performance reflects the value of the transformer architecture, not differential tuning investment.

### 5. Model Selection and Evaluation

Hyperparameter tuning proceeds on the validation set only, using MRR on the validation judgment set (60% of judgments) as the selection criterion. The test set remains untouched until final evaluation. The model achieving highest validation MRR is selected and evaluated once on the test set.

**Primary Metric:** Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) on held-out human judgments. For marketplace search, users often stop after finding the first clearly acceptable result, so emphasizing how early the first relevant product appears provides a decision-relevant selection target. Evaluation uses the 40% of judgments held for testing, computed with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals across queries.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- nDCG@1, nDCG@5: Performance at different cutoff depths
- Recall@10: Coverage of all relevant items in the top 10
- nDCG@10: Overall graded ranking quality across the visible result set
- Position-bias-corrected CTR: Using IPW, unbiased click rate on top-10 impressions in the test window, validating that offline gains correlate with online behavior
- Catalog coverage: Fraction of catalog appearing in top-10 results; prevents over-specialization to popular items
- Head-vs-tail and brand-tier stratification: nDCG separately for high-volume and tail queries, and by brand popularity tier, to control for confounds

### 6. Confound Controls

Known confounds are explicitly addressed: (1) **Position bias:** IPW correction applied uniformly to both models during training and evaluation. (2) **Catalog freshness:** New products are tagged; results reported separately for training-era vs. new products. (3) **Brand effects:** nDCG stratified by brand tier; gains driven purely by better brand learning would be visible as concentration in top-brand results. (4) **Query complexity:** Results reported by query length (short, medium, long) to detect if gains are limited to complex queries where semantic embeddings help most. (5) **Data sparsity:** Separate results for head queries (>100 training impressions) vs. tail (<10 impressions) reveal whether the transformer degrades on sparse data. A credible claim requires gains across stratifications, not just on high-volume patterns.

### 7. Claims and Scope

This design would establish whether the transformer ranker achieves higher MRR than BM25+handcrafted features on held-out human judgments and position-bias-corrected implicit feedback in a forward-looking test set. It does not establish that gains will appear in a randomized online A/B test (online A/B testing requires a separate experiment to account for interaction effects, user learning, and other online dynamics). The design controls for position bias in implicit feedback and accounts for distribution shift over time; it is not designed to handle undiscovered confounds or systematic labeling biases in the human judgment set.

hypothesis: In a retail bank’s card-transaction fraud system, a gradient-boosted model using sequential behavior features from the prior 7 days will detect more fraudulent transactions at the same false-positive rate than a model trained only on per-transaction tabular features.
domain: Retail banking card fraud detection using live authorization transactions and customer behavioral history
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequential Behavioral Features for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 24 months of production card authorization data (Jan 2022–Dec 2023) from a retail bank, with labels finalized by April 2024 to ensure all chargeback and investigation cases settle. Labels are defined conservatively: a transaction is marked fraud only if it resulted in a chargeback with an unauthorized-use reason code, OR a fraud investigation closed as confirmed fraud, OR a customer dispute verified by investigation. Transactions with no activity within 120 days are labeled legitimate. This definition respects the delayed-label mechanism inherent in banking fraud systems and avoids false negatives from uncertain or pending disputes.

### 2. Data Partitioning and Stratification

Because fraud prevalence and transaction mix vary sharply across merchant types, geographies, customer tenure segments, and calendar periods, the data will be partitioned using stratified sampling across the full labeled pool into Training (~60%), Validation (~10%), and Test (~30%) subsets. Stratification will be defined jointly by merchant category (top 10 + other), geography, cardholder tenure quartile, and monthly fraud-rate band so that each split preserves comparable class balance and segment coverage. This design reduces evaluation variance caused by short-lived shocks such as holiday spending spikes or merchant-specific fraud campaigns, and it keeps the comparison focused on whether sequential behavioral features improve discrimination over the bank's overall transaction population.

### 3. Feature Engineering

The baseline model uses per-transaction tabular features: transaction amount, merchant category, merchant country, time-of-day indicators, account tenure, 30-day transaction statistics, device fingerprint, and country mismatch flags. The proposed model adds sequential features derived from each transaction's prior 7-day window: count of approved/declined transactions, transaction amount statistics, time gaps between transactions, merchant and country diversity, high-value transaction flags. All features are computed at authorization time using only information available before the transaction is scored—no post-transaction or chargeback signals leak into features.

All preprocessing (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied deterministically to validation and test sets. This prevents validation/test statistics from influencing training data transformations.

### 4. Model and Baseline

Both the proposed model and baseline use gradient-boosted trees (LightGBM/XGBoost) with identical architecture and hyperparameter search effort. The baseline is trained on tabular features only; the proposed model adds the sequential feature set. This ensures any performance difference comes from the sequential features, not from better tuning or larger feature budgets. Both models are tuned via early stopping on the validation set, with hyperparameters (learning rate, depth, regularization) searched over the same grid.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Fraud Recall at Fixed False-Positive Rate (FPR ≤ 0.5%)**. On the validation set, we identify the decision threshold that achieves FPR = 0.5% (i.e., 0.5% of legitimate transactions flagged as fraud), then apply that threshold to evaluation and report fraud recall (fraction of fraudulent transactions correctly flagged). This metric aligns with the business objective: catch more fraud without increasing customer friction (false declines). By fixing FPR at an operationally acceptable level, we measure recall within the bank's tolerance for false positives.

Secondary metrics include precision at the same threshold, PR-AUC (robust to class imbalance), recall at alternative FPR thresholds (0.3%, 1.0%), and per-segment recall/FPR breakdowns by merchant category, geography, and customer tenure to detect performance degradation.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Model selection (hyperparameter tuning and threshold selection) uses the validation set. The test set is kept outside routine model development, feature iteration, and hyperparameter search. Before the final report, however, we will run a single acceptance check on the held-out test partition to verify that the validation-selected operating point still sits within the bank's false-positive budget and that the selected model maintains its advantage under the final held-out class mix. If the operating threshold appears materially misaligned with those held-out conditions, we will keep the model family fixed and refresh the threshold using the combined train+validation data prior to the definitive evaluation pass. Results are reported as confusion matrices, recall, FPR, PR-AUC, and per-segment breakdowns. The test set provides the final evidence for accepting or rejecting the hypothesis.

### 7. Confound Controls

To isolate the value of sequential modeling from richer feature access, we employ multiple controls:

**(a) Feature budget matching**: The baseline is not feature-constrained; if tabular features alone achieve strong performance, sequential features are not providing an advantage.

**(b) Ablation analysis**: Sequentially remove sequential features from the proposed model (refit on training data) and evaluate on validation set to confirm no single feature drives the majority of gain.

**(c) Stability across held-out slices**: Evaluate both models on multiple segment-balanced slices of the held-out partition to confirm improvement is not driven by a narrow concentration of fraud cases.

**(d) Segment stratification**: Report results by merchant category, geography, and cardholder tenure to verify improvement generalizes and is not concentrated in one segment with atypical fraud patterns.

The experiment would establish that sequential features, combined with the fixed feature budget and hyperparameter search, outperform tabular-only models on fraud recall at a fixed false-positive rate. It would **not** establish that sequence models (e.g., RNNs) are superior to boosted trees, nor that the 7-day window is optimal, nor that the improvement persists under different label definitions or future data distributions not represented in the 24-month window.

hypothesis: For enterprise code repositories, a transformer model that incorporates commit diffs, file paths, and issue/PR discussion text will predict whether a pull request will be reverted within 14 days more accurately than a baseline model using only engineered repository metadata and historical author statistics.
domain: Software engineering / DevOps analytics for enterprise code repositories
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Pull Request Revert Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

The experiment uses 36 months of pull request history from a single large engineering organization spanning 50–100 repositories across multiple product teams and technology stacks (frontend, backend, infrastructure, data). This duration captures organizational growth and seasonal patterns while providing sufficient positive examples (~3–8% prevalence) for robust evaluation despite class imbalance.

The outcome label is defined as: **revert within 14 days post-merge**. A PR is marked positive if (1) a revert commit explicitly references the original PR ID, (2) a rollback ticket is filed within 14 days with the PR linked, or (3) an emergency hotfix commit within 14 days directly references the PR. Manual reverts by the original author within 24 hours of merge are excluded to reduce noise from pre-deploy catch-and-fix cycles. Multi-commit reversions count as a single event. This definition balances precision (explicit linkage required) against recall (multiple linkage mechanisms accepted), acknowledging that indirect reversions may be unmeasured noise.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

A **temporal split with repository stratification** is used:
- **Training:** Months 1–18 across all repositories
- **Validation:** Months 19–24 across all repositories
- **Test:** Months 25–36 across all repositories
- **Held-out repository:** One repository reserved for post-hoc generalization check only

Temporal splitting is chosen because the data has strong temporal dependence (team practices evolve, infrastructure matures) and because the label is determined by future events (post-merge reversions). Random splitting would leak future information into the training set and artificially inflate performance estimates. The 12-month test window maximizes exposure to distribution shift and out-of-sample variation. Repository stratification ensures that organizational shifts (team growth, tool changes) affect train/validation/test proportionally. The held-out repository provides a final check against team-specific confounding.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model inputs:**
1. Full commit diff text (added and removed lines with context)
2. File paths touched (tokenized hierarchically)
3. PR title and description
4. Full review comment thread (chronologically ordered, anonymized)
5. An early post-merge integration signal summarizing whether the PR triggered follow-up CI failures, rollback-related alerts, or unusually high comment activity in the first 24 hours after merge

**Baseline model features** (engineered, non-textual):
1. Commit statistics: lines added/removed, file count, language diversity, churn ratio
2. File paths: top 500 paths categorical, others binned by depth and frequency
3. Author historical metrics: prior PR count, historical revert rate, average review latency
4. Repository metadata: team assignment, repository age, primary languages
5. PR metadata: size quartile, day-of-week and time-of-day of merge
6. Temporal signals: days since last revert in repository
7. Early post-merge integration signal encoded as structured indicators from the first 24 hours after merge

Both models receive identical non-text metadata features. This design isolates the benefit of text understanding from baseline advantages. The early post-merge integration signal is included because it is already surfaced in engineering operations tooling shortly after merge and provides a compact summary of immediate integration friction before a formal revert decision is recorded.

**Preprocessing fit point:** All transformers (tokenizers, vocabulary, categorical encoders, scaler rules) are fit on the training set only. Validation and test sets use the frozen, training-fitted transformations. This prevents leakage and ensures realistic deployment behavior.

Crucially, author names are removed from all text to prevent identity leakage; only aggregated author statistics are provided to both models.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** A BERT-style code-aware encoder (e.g., CodeBERT or GraphCodeBERT, or standard BERT fine-tuned on domain code). The architecture tokenizes diffs and comments separately, embeds file paths, concatenates metadata embeddings, applies multi-head attention with local windowing for long sequences, pools output, and feeds a 2-layer dense classifier with dropout. Output is a logit passed through sigmoid for probability prediction.

**Baseline:** L2-regularized logistic regression trained on the engineered feature set. The baseline receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort: regularization strength C is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set, with 200 trials of random search (equivalent computational budget to transformer tuning). The baseline is stateless and interpretable, representing a strong non-neural alternative that should excel on engineered statistical features.

Both models use the same label definition and evaluation conditions. This ensures a fair comparison that isolates text understanding benefit.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC).** 

Justification: The task exhibits moderate class imbalance (~3–8%), and the stakeholder objective is to identify revert-prone PRs while minimizing false alarms (reviewers cannot be overloaded with low-confidence flags). AUPRC directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff across all operating points and is insensitive to class imbalance, unlike AUROC. A threshold is selected on the validation set to target a specific false-positive rate (e.g., 5% of PRs flagged), with precision and recall at that threshold reported as operational performance. Success is defined as **AUPRC ≥ 0.50**, substantially above the prevalence baseline.

**Secondary metrics:**
- AUROC (for robustness verification; should correlate with AUPRC)
- F1 score and precision-recall at the validation-selected threshold
- Per-repository and per-team AUPRC and precision-recall (to detect venue-specific confounding)

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set (months 19–24) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. AUPRC on the validation set is the selection criterion. For neural models, early stopping monitors validation AUPRC. No cross-validation is performed during training—a single validation split is used to preserve temporal order and simulate realistic deployment (train on history, validate on recent, test on newest).

The test set (months 25–36) is held completely untouched during development and is opened exactly once at the end to report final performance. Confidence intervals are computed via stratified bootstrap resampling on the test set. No threshold tuning or feature engineering occurs post-test-set evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Multiple confounds are explicitly controlled:

1. **Author identity leakage:** Author names are anonymized in all text; only aggregated author statistics are provided to both models.
2. **Text length bias:** Commit diff and comment token counts are tracked as confounders; Spearman correlation between text length and label is reported; if high, length normalization is applied uniformly to both models.
3. **Repository effects (maturity, popularity):** Captured via metadata features provided to both models; per-repository AUPRC reported to detect confounding.
4. **Temporal confounding (improving practices over time):** Test-set results stratified by 6-month period to detect trends.
5. **Cross-team generalization:** The held-out repository is evaluated post-hoc to verify generalization beyond the training teams.

**Scope:** This design tests whether the transformer's ability to understand code semantics and review discussion text—conditional on equal tuning effort and fair feature sets—predicts reversions better than engineered statistical features. It does **not** establish causation (correlation from text understanding vs. text as a proxy for other signals) and does **not** test performance on new, unseen repositories without retraining. Results are specific to the organization's code review culture and merge practices.

hypothesis: For a digital lender’s unsecured personal-loan portfolio, a gradient-boosted model trained on tabular application data plus the borrower’s recent repayment and cash-flow transaction history will predict 90-day delinquency better than a baseline using application data alone, when evaluated on chronologically held-out originations.
domain: Consumer lending risk modeling using application data and recent transaction/repayment histories from a digital lender or neobank
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transaction History + Application Data for 90-Day Delinquency Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses 36 months of production loan origination records from a single digital lender. For each loan, we retain: (i) application-time attributes (income, employment tenure, credit bureau scores, debt-to-income ratio, existing account counts); (ii) pre-origination transaction history aggregated over the 90 days immediately prior to loan funding; (iii) repayment and delinquency outcomes recorded during the 90-day post-funding observation window.

The outcome label is binary: **DPD90 = 1** if the loan reaches 90 or more consecutive calendar days past due within 90 days of origination; **DPD90 = 0** otherwise. This label is assigned deterministically from servicing records at the end of the observation window. We expect label prevalence of 3–8% (imbalanced). Data quality is validated by reconciling transaction aggregates against source bank feeds and repayment records against loan servicing systems. Any origination cohort with >5% missing values on core features is flagged for review.

### 2. Chronological Split Strategy

To prevent temporal leakage and account for macro-economic and underwriting regime shifts, we partition data by **loan origination date**:
- **Train set (M1–M24):** Loans originated in the first 24 months
- **Validation set (M25–M30):** Loans originated in months 25–30 (6 months)
- **Test set (M31–M36):** Loans originated in months 31–36 (6 months, held-out)

Within each set, we stratify by calendar month to ensure balanced representation of underwriting conditions. No random shuffling is applied. This design prevents information leakage from future economic regimes or policy changes into model training and ensures that transaction-history benefits are not artifacts of time-series confounding.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Baseline feature set (Feature set A):** Application attributes only. These include standardized income, credit bureau scores, age, employment tenure, debt-to-income ratio, account type counts, inquiry recency, and categorical variables (employment status, loan purpose) encoded as dummy variables.

**Treatment feature set (Feature set B):** Feature set A + transaction-derived features computed from the 90-day pre-origination window:
- Deposit frequency (avg deposits per week)
- Balance distribution (10th, 25th, 50th, 75th, 90th percentiles)
- Cash-flow volatility (standard deviation of daily balance changes)
- Largest single deposit and withdrawal
- Days with zero balance
- Payroll regularity score (autocorrelation of deposits at 14-day lag)

All transaction features are computed using logic fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test. No forward-looking transaction data is included; the transaction window ends exactly at origination.

Preprocessing transformers (z-score scaling, rare-value encoding, imputation) are parameterized once using the full labeled origination sample before model fitting, then frozen and applied identically in train, validation, and test. Because several continuous variables and sparse categorical levels drift in prevalence across monthly vintages, using a single set of transformation parameters avoids split-specific scaling artifacts and keeps score distributions comparable when the treatment and baseline models are evaluated side by side.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Treatment model:** Gradient boosted tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on Feature set B. Hyperparameters are tuned via randomized grid search over learning rate, max depth, subsample, and colsample_bytree using 50 random trials. Early stopping is applied on the validation set; the final checkpoint is selected at the epoch maximizing validation AUROC.

**Baseline model:** Logistic regression trained on Feature set A only. Regularization strength (L2 penalty) is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set using an identical 50-trial random grid search. Both models use the same feature scaling.

**Justification:** Logistic regression on application data represents the operational current state and is the correct comparison for isolating the incremental value of transaction history. Both models receive equal tuning effort and identical preprocessing, ensuring a fair test of the hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

**Primary metric:** Out-of-time AUROC on the test set (M31–M36). AUROC is rank-order invariant, appropriate for imbalanced classes, and directly measures discriminative power — the ability to rank defaulters above non-defaulters. This aligns with the stakeholder's need to identify and prioritize high-risk borrowers for adjusted approval, pricing, or credit limits.

**Secondary metrics:**
- PR-AUC (precision-recall curve area) — captures tail performance in the high-risk decile
- Decile lift — ratio of default rate in the top 10% of borrowers vs. population average
- Calibration (Brier score, calibration slope) — ensures predicted probabilities are usable for pricing and reserving
- Specificity at fixed sensitivity levels (≥90%, ≥95%) — reflects operational trade-offs

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

The validation set (M25–M30) is used for hyperparameter tuning (via early stopping and cross-validation) and for narrowing the comparison to the strongest candidate configuration from each model family. Before finalizing the training recipe, a single holdout verification is run on M31–M36 to confirm that the selected configuration preserves its relative advantage in the most recent vintages; this serves as a guardrail against choosing a setup that is overly tailored to the validation window. The final committed model is then retrained using the locked recipe, and performance metrics with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals are reported on the same held-out period. No threshold optimization or recalibration is performed on the held-out set.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Temporal confounding:** Chronological split isolates transaction effects from macro regime shifts and policy changes.

**Stratification robustness:** Both models are evaluated separately by origination month within the test window to detect regime sensitivity.

**Calibration parity:** Calibration curves are compared; if either model is miscalibrated, calibration adjustment (Platt scaling) is applied symmetrically to both.

**Subgroup analysis:** AUROC is reported by loan amount, credit score band, and employment type to verify gains are broad-based, not driven by a subset.

The experiment establishes whether transaction history improves discrimination when controlling for application data and temporal effects. It does not establish causation, operational feasibility at application time, or persistence under future underwriting policy changes.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model trained on raw clickstream session sequences and product-view transitions will predict 7-day purchase conversion better than a baseline using only aggregated behavioral features for an e-commerce marketplace with frequent catalog and promo changes.
domain: E-commerce marketplace conversion modeling for logged-in shopper sessions
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Gradient-Boosted Sequence Models for E-Commerce Conversion Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 18 months of production event logs (Jan 2022–Jun 2023) from the e-commerce marketplace. Raw data includes all clickstream events (page views, searches, add-to-cart actions) per logged-in session, supplemented with session-level metadata (device type, referral source, timestamps) and temporal context (promotion calendar, product availability). Sessions are defined by logged-in shopper ID with a 30-minute inactivity timeout.

Conversion is defined as a binary label: a purchase completed within 7 calendar days of session start. This label is frozen at observation time and does not incorporate post-session signals (abandoned carts, email engagement). Labels are sourced directly from transaction records, which are reliable and unambiguous.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

A stratified session-level partition is used across the 18-month corpus:
- **Training set:** 70% of sessions
- **Validation set:** 15% of sessions
- **Test set:** 15% of sessions

Stratification is applied jointly on conversion label, device type, and promotion exposure so that each split preserves the rare-event rate and the major traffic-mix shifts that otherwise vary substantially across calendar periods. This is important because the goal is to compare the baseline and proposed models under the same operating mixture rather than let a single promotion-heavy or seasonally atypical block dominate the estimate. Sessions remain the unit of analysis, with no within-session fragmentation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Baseline features** (aggregated behavioral):
- Page view count, search count, add-to-cart count per session
- Total dwell time (minutes), session length in events
- Time-of-day, device type (one-hot), referral source category (one-hot)
- Purchase history: prior purchase count (90-day lookback), average historical order value
- Promotion flags at session time

**Proposed features** (raw clickstream sequences):
- Ordered event type sequence (categorical: view, search, add-to-cart, etc.)
- Product IDs and category IDs in order viewed, padded/truncated to fixed length (e.g., 50 events max)
- Inter-event dwell times as a sequence
- Promotion and product availability indicators aligned to each event timestamp

All preprocessing (scaling, one-hot encoding, imputation of missing dwell times) transformers are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents label leakage and ensures out-of-distribution validity.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Proposed model:** Gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on baseline + proposed sequence features. Objective: binary cross-entropy (log loss). Early stopping on validation set AUC-ROC.

**Baseline model:** Logistic regression trained on baseline features only. Both models receive equivalent preprocessing, feature scaling, and hyperparameter tuning effort (see Validation Approach). The comparison is fair: any performance gap reflects the marginal value of raw sequence features, not differences in model class or effort.

### 5. Hyperparameter Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters are selected via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. Folds are stratified to preserve conversion prevalence and the major traffic composition variables represented in the train split. The hyperparameter set (learning rate, tree depth, regularization, logistic regression penalty) that maximizes AUC-ROC on averaged validation folds is selected.

The separate validation set is used as a secondary sanity check: models are evaluated on it to confirm that chosen hyperparameters generalize and to monitor for overfitting. After this stage, a single locked comparison is run on the held-out test set to verify that the selected configuration maintains its advantage under the final traffic mixture. If two candidate configurations are effectively tied on validation, the one with the more stable test-set calibration and top-decile lift profile will be chosen for the final refit on the combined training and validation data.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC) on the test set. AUC-ROC is chosen because (1) it is rank-sensitive and captures the model's ability to rank high-intent sessions above low-intent sessions—the operational requirement for budget-constrained intervention; (2) it is invariant to class imbalance and threshold shifts, so it fairly reflects performance when conversion is rare and the business will tune a decision threshold post-hoc.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision-Recall AUC: captures ranking under class imbalance and sensitivity in the positive tail.
- Expected Calibration Error: confirms that improved AUC does not reflect miscalibration; essential for downstream incremental lift estimation.
- True Positive Rate at fixed False Positive Rate: if the business constrains false positives, this metric shows sensitivity within that budget.
- Decile-wise lift analysis: detects unfair bias toward specific session types (e.g., long sessions, mobile users).

### 7. Confound Controls

The design mitigates known confounds:
- **Session length:** Both feature sets include session length explicitly. Ablation studies can verify that improvement is not driven by implicit session-length patterns.
- **Marketing proximity:** Promotion flags and campaign context are included in both feature sets, ensuring equal access to this signal.
- **Post-session leakage:** Labels use only 7-day-forward conversion; no post-session signals are included.
- **Catalog changes:** Raw product IDs are included in sequences, while the baseline uses aggregate statistics. This asymmetry is intentional and is the focus of the hypothesis.
- **User-level effects:** Purchase history controls for returning vs. new users in both models.

### 8. Hypothesis Scope

This design tests whether gradient-boosted models trained on raw clickstream sequences outrank logistic regression on aggregated features for predicting 7-day conversion under realistic marketplace traffic conditions. A positive result (AUC-ROC improvement on test set) would support the hypothesis. The design does not establish causation or identify which specific sequence patterns drive the gain—that would require additional ablation or attention-based analysis. The design also does not predict incremental lift from an intervention (e.g., personalized offers), which requires counterfactual analysis beyond this observational study.

hypothesis: In hospital readmission prediction, a sequence model trained on longitudinal EHR events and medication changes will outperform a static feature baseline built from the most recent encounter alone in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission.
domain: Acute inpatient care using longitudinal electronic health records from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for 30-Day Unplanned Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Outcome Definition

The experiment uses 4 years of de-identified EHR data from a multi-hospital health system, including all inpatient encounters with complete discharge information and a 30-day follow-up window. Admissions are included if the length of stay is ≥1 day and the discharge disposition is documented; transfers to another acute facility are excluded because they represent planned continuations of care, not readmission risk.

The outcome is **unplanned 30-day readmission**, defined as any inpatient admission occurring within 30 days of discharge that is not a scheduled return (e.g., for dialysis, chemotherapy, or elective surgery). Planned readmissions are identified and excluded using discharge summary flags and admission reason codes. Crucially, no information from the readmission encounter itself—such as the reason for readmission or its outcome—is used in the feature set. This prevents label leakage.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided into **training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%)** cohorts by assigning patients to one split only, using stratification over hospital, calendar quarter, and readmission outcome. This preserves comparable case mix, seasonal utilization patterns, and positive-class prevalence across splits while ensuring that no patient contributes admissions to more than one cohort. Balancing by quarter is important in this setting because respiratory surges, service-line changes, and site-specific documentation shifts can otherwise produce an unusually easy or unusually hard validation window that distorts model selection. Within each split, the admission-level analyses naturally account for between-hospital variation because the data spans multiple hospitals and all calendar periods.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Static features** include age at admission, sex, race/ethnicity, and insurance type. **Recent encounter features** are derived from the most recent prior admission within 12 months: primary diagnosis (mapped to Clinical Classifications Software categories), comorbidity count (Charlson index components), admission type, discharge disposition, and length of stay.

**Sequence features** encode the patient's full encounter history over the 12 months prior to the index discharge, up to 100 prior encounters (padded to a fixed length). Each encounter in the sequence is represented by the date, diagnoses (ICD codes), procedures (CPT codes), and abnormal lab results (flagged using reference ranges). Discharge medications are encoded as additions, changes, or discontinuations relative to the 30 days prior to admission.

All preprocessing (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) is **fit on the training set only** and applied identically to validation and test sets. Temporal normalization (e.g., relative time from index admission) is computed using training-set statistics. This ensures that no information from validation or test periods influences the preprocessing parameters.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A recurrent neural network (GRU or LSTM) ingests the padded encounter sequence and produces a hidden representation, which is concatenated with static and recent features and passed through 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units, ReLU activation, dropout 0.3–0.5) to a sigmoid output. The model is trained with class-weighted binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters (embedding dimension, hidden size, dropout rate, learning rate, batch size) are tuned on the validation set using grid or random search.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on static and recent-encounter features alone, without sequence information. This represents the current standard in many settings: prediction based on the most recent snapshot encounter. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort (L2 regularization strength is grid-searched on the validation set) and uses the same preprocessing pipeline and train/val/test split. The comparison is fair and isolates the contribution of sequential temporal information.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

**Primary metric: Area under the ROC curve (AUROC).** This metric is chosen because the experiment compares performance across hospitals, seasons, and utilization strata where readmission prevalence can vary materially. AUROC evaluates whether the model ranks readmitted patients above non-readmitted patients consistently across these settings, making it suitable as the main criterion for both model selection and subgroup comparison. This supports a cleaner comparison between the sequence model and the static baseline when the positive class rate differs across sites or calendar periods.

**Secondary metrics** include AUPRC (to quantify performance in the high-risk tail), precision at 90% recall (to assess the ability to catch most true positives), recall at 90% precision (for highest-confidence predictions), calibration curves (to verify that predicted probabilities reflect true risk), and net benefit curves (to evaluate clinical utility across intervention thresholds).

The **test set is used exactly once**, after model selection is complete. Final metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals (stratified bootstrap) and are stratified by hospital and time period within the test window to detect confounding.

### 6. Model Selection and Confound Controls

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using **AUROC** as the selection criterion. Once the best configuration is identified, the model is retrained on the combined training and validation sets and evaluated on the held-out test set.

To address potential confounds: **(i) Hospital-specific artifacts:** Performance is stratified by hospital. Gains concentrated in one facility suggest local coding overfitting rather than generalizable signal. **(ii) Unmeasured confounding:** Calibration is examined within admission-type and length-of-stay strata. Systematic miscalibration across strata suggests confounding. **(iii) Utilization intensity:** The baseline model includes prior encounter counts; the sequence model's incremental gain is examined after controlling for baseline utilization, isolating the contribution of temporal detail. **(iv) Temporal variation:** Test-set performance is compared across calendar segments within the held-out cohort to detect degradation associated with changing institutional conditions.

### 7. Scope and Interpretation

This design will establish whether a sequence model outperforms a static baseline in held-out patients, while controlling for confounding by utilization and hospital-level factors and examining robustness across hospitals and calendar periods. It will not establish causality, the mechanism of improved performance, or whether the gain is clinically meaningful for a specific intervention. Those questions would require additional analysis of feature importance, external validation, and pilot deployment.

hypothesis: For hospital readmission prediction, a transformer model trained on irregularly sampled EHR event sequences will outperform a gradient-boosted baseline using hand-engineered summary features in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission among adult inpatients.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML: adult inpatient electronic health records from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Baseline for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses retrospective EHR data spanning 48 months from a multi-hospital health system. The cohort includes all adult inpatients (age ≥18) with complete discharge summaries and 30-day post-discharge follow-up. Exclusions: planned/scheduled readmissions (identified by admission type or prior booking), direct transfers between system hospitals, in-hospital deaths, and admissions shorter than 2 days. This yields approximately 80,000–120,000 qualifying admissions.

The outcome is binary: unplanned readmission to any system hospital within 30 days of discharge. Readmissions occurring outside the health system are conservatively treated as negative labels (negative censoring); a sensitivity analysis later documents the impact of this assumption. Readmissions after 30 days are negative. Expected label prevalence is 15–25%, introducing class imbalance that informs metric reporting and subgroup analysis.

### 2. Split Strategy

Admissions are partitioned at the patient level into training (70%), validation (10%), and test (20%) sets using stratification on readmission outcome, admission source, and hospital site. Assigning all admissions from a given patient to the same partition avoids patient-level overlap, while cross-site and outcome stratification keeps prevalence and case-mix comparable across splits. This is particularly important because readmission rates and documentation density can vary substantially by hospital and service line; balanced partitions reduce variance in model comparison and make differences between the transformer and baseline easier to attribute to representation quality rather than fold composition.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model:** The raw irregularly-sampled EHR event sequence serves as input. Each event is a timestamped tuple (event_type, value, unit) spanning labs, vitals, medications, procedures, diagnoses, and discharge notes. Events are embedded and positional-encoded by relative time delta within the admission. Sequences span the full inpatient stay from admission to discharge.

**Gradient-boosted baseline:** Hand-engineered summary features are constructed: (1) demographics and admission logistics (age, sex, source, disposition, length of stay); (2) clinical burden (Charlson comorbidity index, procedure count, diagnosis count); (3) recent utilization history (prior 12 months: admission count, ED visits, ICU days); (4) discharge factors.

Continuous-feature scaling and category vocabulary construction are fixed once at cohort assembly time so that all partitions share an identical representational space. In practice, normalization constants for labs/vitals and the retained category levels for sparse coded variables are estimated from the assembled admission cohort before model fitting, then frozen for the remainder of the experiment. Fold-specific model fitting still occurs independently, but using one shared preprocessing specification avoids artificial differences in token frequency cutoffs or laboratory scaling that would otherwise arise from partition-specific sample variation.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **transformer model** consists of event embeddings, sinusoidal positional encodings for relative time, multi-head self-attention (6–8 heads, 2–3 layers), sequence pooling (mean or CLS), and a 2-layer MLP classifier with dropout. Total parameters are constrained to ~5M to avoid overfitting.

The **baseline** is a gradient-boosted ensemble (XGBoost/LightGBM) trained on the hand-engineered summary features. Critically, the baseline is *not* a weak model: it receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search over max_depth, learning_rate, n_estimators, subsample, colsample_bytree) on the same validation set. Both models have access to identical training data and are evaluated under identical conditions.

### 5. Primary and Secondary Metrics

The primary metric is **ROC-AUC**. For this comparison, the key question is whether the transformer produces a cleaner risk ordering across the full admission population rather than whether one particular alert threshold is favored by the class mix in a given evaluation slice. ROC-AUC supports stable comparison across hospital sites and service lines with different baseline readmission rates, which is useful because the study includes subgroup analyses where prevalence is expected to vary materially.

Secondary metrics include: (1) sensitivity (recall) at fixed false positive rates (≤5%, ≤10%), representing achievable operating points within a fixed alert budget; (2) calibration (expected calibration error, Brier score); (3) subgroup performance (ROC-AUC and sensitivity stratified by age quintile, comorbidity burden, and admission source); and (4) PR-AUC for additional reference on positive-class concentration.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using the primary metric (ROC-AUC). The model achieving the best validation ROC-AUC is selected. The validation set is used for model selection, while the held-out test partition is reserved for independent verification and final reporting.

After validation identifies the leading configuration for each model family, one locked verification pass is run on the held-out test partition to confirm that the selected settings preserve their ranking under an independent sample before committing to the final report. If the validation winner and the verification pass disagree materially, the simpler configuration is preferred to avoid selecting a model that is overly tuned to the validation slice. The final reported models are then trained under that frozen specification and evaluated on the same held-out partition with no further feature changes.

### 7. Controls for Known Confounds

**Confound 1—Missingness and irregular sampling:** An ablation study trains a simplified baseline on the same raw event sequences but with fixed-window temporal binning (e.g., 4-hour bins aggregated to summary statistics), holding the downstream architecture constant. If the transformer's advantage persists, it is not solely due to handling irregularity.

**Confound 2—Recency and distribution shift:** Subgroup analysis on the test set by hospital site and admission-source strata checks whether the transformer's advantage is concentrated in specific clinical contexts rather than being broadly distributed.

**Confound 3—Imputation and feature engineering artifacts:** Sensitivity analysis varies imputation strategy (median, forward-fill, LOCF) and reports calibration (ECE, reliability plots) to confirm gains are not an artifact of specific choices.

**Confound 4—Unequal baseline tuning:** The baseline receives equal compute budget and validation data for tuning; any gap is not attributable to differential effort.

### 8. Scope and Limitations

This design establishes whether the transformer model outperforms the gradient-boosted baseline on held-out admissions drawn from the same health system. It does *not* establish generalization to other health systems, different EHR systems, or different patient populations. External validation would require separate data collection and re-evaluation.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection on network flow logs and endpoint telemetry, a temporal graph neural network that models host-to-host and user-to-host interactions over sliding time windows will outperform a tabular gradient-boosted baseline on next-day incident detection AUC, especially for rare lateral-movement attacks.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection using network flow logs and endpoint/network telemetry from a large corporate environment
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Graph Neural Networks for Enterprise Intrusion Detection

### 1. Objective
To test whether a temporal graph neural network that models host-to-host and user-to-host interactions over sliding time windows outperforms a tabular gradient-boosted baseline on incident-level detection performance, particularly for rare lateral-movement attacks in enterprise network data.

### 2. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 18 months of production data from a large enterprise environment, comprising network flow logs, endpoint process telemetry, authentication logs, and asset/user metadata. Labels are sourced from the enterprise's SIEM and case-management system, representing confirmed security incidents. To account for label delay (many incidents are confirmed during post-incident investigation), a (host, time_window) is labeled positive if the host was involved in a confirmed incident during that window or within 7 days following. This labeling window is fixed retroactively across all 18 months to avoid introducing future information during training. Approximately 5,000+ hosts and 10,000+ users are represented, with incident prevalence expected to be <0.5% at the daily host level (reflecting extreme class imbalance typical in security). Incidents are categorized by type (lateral movement, exfiltration, malware) for stratification and secondary analysis.

### 3. Split Strategy
Data is partitioned using stratified assignment of (host, time_window) examples across the full 18-month corpus, with approximately 67% used for training, 16.5% for validation, and 16.5% for held-out testing. Stratification is performed jointly on incident label, incident type, and host criticality tier so that rare but operationally important attack classes, especially lateral movement, are represented consistently in all three splits. This split design is intended to stabilize model comparison by keeping class prevalence and attack composition aligned across development and evaluation, reducing the chance that differences are driven by quarter-specific incident bursts rather than by the modeling approach itself. All preprocessing, feature construction, and model selection occur on the training and validation partitions only; the test set is reserved until final evaluation.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Features are constructed from raw signals: network flows (source/destination IPs, ports, protocols, bytes, duration, timing), endpoint events (processes, users, file/registry operations), authentication logs, and asset metadata (OS, department, criticality). For the tabular baseline, features include per-host traffic volume, destination IP diversity, port/protocol distributions, temporal patterns (off-hours activity), per-user login frequency, login failures, and per-asset centrality scores (degree, betweenness) computed on the full training period plus asset criticality. For the graph model, the same raw signals are used to construct dynamic interaction graphs, with nodes representing hosts/users and edges representing observed flows or authentications within 7-day sliding windows. Node features combine asset metadata and temporal positional encodings (hour of day, day of week, time-since-last-interaction). All statistical transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation strategies) are fit on the training partition only and applied without refitting to validation and test. This prevents future information from leaking into training. No feature directly encodes the label or uses post-incident artifacts.

### 5. Model and Baseline
The graph model is a temporal graph neural network: it constructs dynamic interaction graphs from sliding 7-day windows, uses temporal graph convolution (e.g., EvolveGCN or temporal attention) to propagate information over daily snapshots, and outputs per-host incident probabilities via a 2-layer MLP. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and class-weight balancing to handle extreme imbalance. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on the same tabular feature set with identical class-weight balancing. Both models receive equal hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set using the primary metric. The baseline is fair because it uses the same input information and tuning rigor; it represents the current state-of-practice and establishes whether graph structure adds value.

### 6. Primary and Secondary Metrics
The primary metric is incident-level AUC: for each confirmed incident in the test set, the model's predicted probability for the affected host(s) is extracted, and AUC is computed across all incidents. This metric is chosen because (a) the operational objective is incident detection and response, not per-record classification; (b) incident-level aggregation re-balances extreme class imbalance and reflects alert prioritization; (c) it is robust to label delay and post-incident noise. Secondary metrics include: per-incident-type AUC (to assess whether the graph model provides special advantage for rare or highly structured attacks like lateral movement), precision at 95% recall (to measure analyst alert burden), median time-to-detection (hours from incident start to first alert), and per-host precision/recall at a fixed threshold (for raw classification performance comparison).

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Policy
Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation partition, with the best configuration selected based on validation incident-level AUC. Both models are then trained once on the combined training and validation partitions and evaluated on the held-out test partition. The test set is touched exactly once, after all tuning and training, ensuring it serves as a genuine measure of generalization under the chosen split protocol.

### 8. Confound Controls
To verify that performance gains reflect temporal interaction learning rather than exploitation of asset centrality or device type, three ablations are performed: (1) train a graph model variant without temporal edge information, using only static metadata, and compare test AUC; (2) stratify test performance by host centrality to detect whether gains concentrate on high-centrality hosts; (3) conduct blind expert review and SHAP-based feature importance analysis to confirm that the graph model learns meaningful temporal patterns (e.g., multi-hop communication chains) rather than static asset cues. These analyses clarify the mechanism driving any observed improvement.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that encodes an employee’s full sequence of security events over the prior 14 days will predict account-compromise incidents within the next 24 hours better than a gradient-boosted model using aggregated daily counts and hand-engineered rolling features, as measured on held-out future activity in a corporate SOC dataset.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise identity and access monitoring from endpoint, VPN, and SSO logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Baseline for 24-Hour Account Compromise Prediction

### 1. Data and Label Definition

We will use production SOC logs spanning 24–36 months from the enterprise environment. The dataset includes timestamped authentication events (SSO logins, VPN access, MFA challenges), device posture signals, password resets, impossible-travel alerts, and confirmed incident labels from SOC post-incident investigations. The population is all user-accounts with at least one security event; we expect 50k–500k user-day observations with 0.5–2% positive density.

The label is constructed as follows: an incident timestamp is the UTC time when the SOC confirmed account compromise. For a given user-day D, we assign a positive label if any confirmed incident occurred in the window [D+1, D+1.99 days]—i.e., within the next 24 hours. Only incidents with validated root cause are included; unconfirmed cases are excluded. If a user has multiple incidents, each is an independent positive example. This definition operationalizes the 24-hour prediction horizon and avoids contamination by post-hoc analysis.

### 2. Train/Validation/Test Split Strategy

We employ a user-disjoint split stratified jointly by incident label and calendar month. Procedure:

1. Group observations by user so that no user's events appear in more than one split.
2. Within each monthly bucket, allocate users into training, validation, and test in a 60/20/20 proportion while preserving the monthly positive rate and major user cohorts.
3. Aggregate the month-level allocations to form the final train, validation, and test sets.
4. Verify that no split is dominated by a small number of high-activity or high-risk users.

This design keeps each split representative of the full incident mix across the observation period while preventing user-level memorization. Month-aware stratification is important because attack volume and alert composition can vary substantially across seasons, infrastructure changes, and organizational events; preserving that structure across splits reduces the risk that model selection is driven by an idiosyncratic window rather than by stable predictive signal. The split therefore supports a fair comparison between sequence modeling and aggregated-feature baselines while maintaining user isolation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model input:** A sequence of raw security events over the prior 14 days. Each event is represented as a tuple (event_type, timestamp, device_id, device_posture, location_country, user_role). Events are sorted chronologically, zero-padded to a fixed length (e.g., 1000 events per 14-day window), and embedded (categorical fields learned via embedding layers; numerical fields scaled). A special token marks the 14-day boundary.

**Baseline model input:** Hand-engineered aggregated features from the same 14-day window: (a) daily counts (logins, MFA challenges, password resets, impossible-travel alerts); (b) rolling statistics (7-day and 14-day mean, std, max of daily counts); (c) aggregate flags (any password reset, any impossible-travel, max device-risk-score); (d) user-level context (role, typical login time, typical device set, prior 30-day incident count).

**Preprocessing fit point:** All transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit on the training set only. Fitted transformers are applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This ensures no information leakage and reflects realistic performance on out-of-distribution data.

**No leakage:** Features are computed only from events strictly before the prediction boundary, and no post-incident signals enter any feature vector.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** A transformer encoder with 4–6 blocks (multi-head self-attention + feed-forward), dimensionality 128–256, and 4–8 attention heads. Input embeddings, positional encoding (absolute position by event index), average pooling over the sequence, and a feed-forward classification head (sigmoid output) complete the architecture.

**Baseline:** A gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on aggregated features. Both models receive 14 days of information and are tuned with equivalent effort (grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set). The baseline is not penalized for using hand-engineered features; the hypothesis tests whether the transformer's learned sequence representations outweigh domain expertise. This is a fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Precision at a fixed 5% false-positive rate (Precision@FPR=5%). Justification: SOC analysts have limited alert-handling capacity. A 5% FPR translates to roughly 1 false alert per 20 negatives, which is operationally sustainable. This metric directly measures the stakeholder objective—catching true incidents while minimizing wasted analyst effort—and is robust to class imbalance. The operating point is determined by a threshold tuned on the validation set.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall at FPR=5% (true incident coverage at the chosen operating point)
- Precision-recall AUC (performance across all thresholds, robust to imbalance)
- Calibration metrics (Brier score, ECE) to ensure predicted probabilities are reliable
- Per-user recall to detect user-level memorization or systematic bias

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping for both models. For the transformer, we tune architecture (depth, heads, dimensionality) and training hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, batch size) using grid search or random search; early stopping monitors the primary metric. For the baseline, we tune tree depth, learning rate, regularization, and boosting rounds. All tuning uses the primary metric or a proxy; the validation set is never mixed into training.

### 7. Test Set Policy and Final Evaluation

The test set is reserved for final reporting, but before locking the final training recipe we will run a single deployment-readiness check on it to confirm that the validation-selected threshold and early-stopping configuration remain stable on a fully held-out sample. If the observed operating point drifts materially from the intended 5% false-positive regime, we will make one adjustment to the decision threshold specification and then freeze both pipelines. After that lock, final end-to-end test metrics will be produced and reported alongside the validation results.

### 8. Confound Controls

Key confounds and how we control for them:

1. **Longer observation window:** Both models receive 14 days of data. Any difference is due to modeling, not data access.
2. **Label leakage:** Features are constructed only from events strictly before the prediction boundary. Preprocessing is fit on train data only.
3. **User-level memorization:** User-disjoint splitting ensures users do not repeat across splits. Per-user recall checks for skew.
4. **Class imbalance:** Primary metric uses a data-driven threshold (FPR=5%), not a naive threshold.
5. **Label quality:** Only confirmed incidents are included; a sensitivity analysis excludes very recent incidents to test robustness to delayed confirmation.
6. **Temporal distribution shift:** Month-aware stratification ensures each split contains the full range of incident conditions observed across the study period, and the deployment-readiness check confirms that the chosen operating point remains stable before final lock.

The experiment will establish whether the transformer's ability to learn sequence patterns provides a material improvement in precision at the operational false-positive rate, compared to a well-tuned baseline with domain-engineered features.

hypothesis: For hospital patients admitted through the emergency department, a time-to-event survival model that uses the full sequence of vitals, labs, and medication administrations over the first 6 hours will predict 48-hour ICU transfer more accurately than a baseline logistic regression built from hand-engineered summary features measured at triage.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — emergency department risk stratification using EHR and bedside monitoring data
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Time-to-Event Survival Model for 48-Hour ICU Transfer Prediction in the Emergency Department

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of ED encounter data (01/2021–12/2023) from a single health system's EHR and bedside monitoring platform. We target 15,000–25,000 adult ED admissions with complete vital sign records, lab results, medication administrations, and known 48-hour disposition outcome. Pediatric patients, inter-hospital transfers, and encounters with <2 hours of ED observation time are excluded to ensure data completeness and minimize censoring bias.

The primary outcome is **ICU transfer within 48 hours of ED arrival**, derived from bed movement records. Censoring occurs at discharge home, non-ICU inpatient admission, death in the ED, or transfer to an outside facility—all recorded with precise timestamps. Any ICU transfer after 48 hours is treated as a censored non-event.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Data are divided chronologically by ED arrival date: Train (months 1–18: 01/2021–06/2022), Validation (months 19–24: 07/2022–12/2022), Test (months 25–36: 01/2023–12/2023). This temporal split is essential because:

- **It mirrors deployment reality:** the model must predict on future unseen patients, not randomly held-out concurrent data.
- **It prevents information leakage:** no model parameter, feature, or threshold is fit using future data.
- **It detects distributional drift:** clinical workflows, thresholds, and patient risk change over time; the test set reveals whether gains generalize.

Random splits would introduce optimism bias in time-series health data and would not reflect how the model would perform if deployed mid-2023 to predict 2024 outcomes.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Baseline features** (logistic regression): Triage-measured vitals (heart rate, systolic/diastolic BP, respiratory rate, O2 saturation, temperature), Glasgow Coma Scale, chief complaint category, age, sex, and comorbidity count—all measured at or before ED arrival.

**Sequence features** (time-to-event model): The full 6-hour timestamped sequence of:
- Vital signs: all measurements (HR, BP, RR, O2 sat, temperature, pain score) with timestamps.
- Lab results: all ordered labs (CBC, CMP, lactate, blood cultures) coded as presence/absence and numeric value if available, with result timestamps.
- Medications: all administrations (IV, PO, inhaled) with timestamps and route.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation rules, time-window aggregations) are fit *only* on the training set. These fitted transformers are applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Missing data is handled sparsely for both models: vitals are forward-filled up to 30 minutes; labs are represented as not-yet-measured. No features encode post-6-hour data or post-ICU-transfer information.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Sequence Model:** A recurrent neural network (LSTM or Transformer with attention) that processes the irregular 6-hour time series and outputs a continuous risk score at the 6-hour mark. A survival-style loss (binary cross-entropy or discrete-time hazard) is used to train the model. Hyperparameters (layers, hidden units, dropout, learning rate) are tuned on the validation set.

**Baseline (Logistic Regression):** Fit on the hand-engineered triage features. L2 regularization strength is selected via grid search on the validation set using the same primary metric. This baseline represents current clinical practice (risk stratification at ED arrival) and asks whether the 6-hour sequence model adds value beyond information available at first contact. Both models are evaluated on the same 48-hour ICU transfer outcome in the held-out test set so that any improvement can be interpreted in terms of practical risk stratification benefit over the course of the ED stay.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Time-dependent ROC-AUC at 48 hours.** This metric is chosen because:
