Split the data chronologically to respect real drift in attacker behavior, infrastructure, and detection content. Use months 1–12 for training, 13–15 for validation, and 16–18 for test. Keep all alerts tied to the same case or incident in one partition, and compute every aggregate feature in a strict point-in-time fashion so no future analyst action or future incident linkage can leak backward. Engineer features from information available at alert creation: detector metadata, vendor severity, source product, MITRE tags, host and user context, asset criticality, recent alert burst statistics by entity and rule, prior incident involvement up to that timestamp, and textual representations of alert title/description. Exclude any post-triage annotations, ticket routing states, and fields only populated after investigation begins. Train a LambdaMART learning-to-rank model, grouping examples by analyst queue-day so the learner optimizes ordering within realistic daily review batches. This directly matches the operating problem: analysts consume a ranked queue, not isolated probability estimates. For a fair test of the hypothesis, compare it against a gradient-boosted binary classifier trained on the identical feature set, with the same temporal partitions and equivalent tuning effort. The classifier’s scores are converted into a ranking at evaluation time, making the comparison about objective formulation rather than model family or feature advantage. Use NDCG@20 as the primary metric, averaged across queue-days. In this setting the SOC does not need a single hard cutoff as much as it needs the most consequential alerts concentrated near the top of a limited review list, and the graded label structure reflects that a confirmed incident is more valuable than a merely suspicious alert. Report Precision@5, Precision@20, MAP, and AUCPR as secondary views, and estimate operational impact with a replay analysis that measures median time from alert generation to first confirmed incident handling under a fixed analyst review budget. Perform model selection on the validation period only, using NDCG@20. Use grouped, chronology-preserving cross-validation inside the training window to narrow hyperparameters, then retrain on train plus validation only after the configuration is fixed. Touch the test set once at the end and report bootstrap confidence intervals over queue-days. Also provide stratified results by detector family, alert source, and asset criticality, plus a held-out slice for rules first introduced in the test period to check that any ranking gain survives realistic drift.

hypothesis: Re] Badder Seeds: Reproducing the Evaluation of Lexical Methods for Bias Measurement
task_prompt:
The original paper studies how lexical seed sets used for NLP bias measurement are constructed and whether their properties affect the behavior of common bias metrics. It first gathers many seed lexicons from prior literature, treating these lexicons as the core objects of analysis. The paper then examines these seed sets both qualitatively, by considering how they were selected and sourced, and quantitatively, by testing how they behave when used in standard bias-measurement procedures.

To run the quantitative analyses, the paper trains word embeddings on multiple corpora and applies established bias measurement methods based on seed words, including approaches associated with WEAT- and PCA-style bias subspaces. Using these embeddings and seed sets, it evaluates whether the induced bias subspaces capture the intended semantic contrast represented by the paired seed terms. It also compares outcomes across alternative seed sets and measurement setups.

A central part of the methodology is to probe the stability of bias measurements under changes to seed-set design. The paper varies factors such as the ordering and pairing of seed terms, similarities among the selected words, and their frequencies in the training data, and then observes how these choices alter the resulting bias subspaces and bias scores. The overall goal is to assess whether commonly reused seed lexicons provide a robust foundation for measuring bias across corpora.

Based on these analyses, the paper argues for more careful validation and justification of seed lexicons before they are reused in bias studies. Its methodology is therefore framed as an evaluation of lexical bias-measurement pipelines, with emphasis on how seed selection decisions influence quantitative conclusions.

hypothesis: Re] Hamiltonian Generative Networks
task_prompt:
The paper proposes Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN), a variational generative model designed to learn the dynamics of simple physical systems directly from high-dimensional image observations. The method encodes observed image sequences into a latent state intended to represent the system in a form suitable for Hamiltonian dynamics. A learned Hamiltonian function is then integrated over time so that the model can reconstruct the temporal evolution of the system and generate trajectories by rolling the latent dynamics forward or backward.

The experimental setup described in the report centers on physical environments such as a pendulum, a body-spring system, and 2-body and 3-body systems. Training data consist of image sequences showing the evolution of these systems. The model is trained as a variational model, balancing a reconstruction objective with a KL regularization term, so that it can both reconstruct observed sequences and sample plausible new trajectories from the learned dynamics.

The claimed capabilities of the approach include reconstructing system trajectories from image observations, learning dynamics without strong domain-specific assumptions, and performing rollout prediction in both temporal directions by integrating the learned Hamiltonian. The evaluation therefore focuses on how well the model captures the evolution of these simulated physical systems from visual input and whether the learned latent dynamics support realistic trajectory generation.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner severity signals will prioritize truly exploitable hosts more effectively than ranking by CVSS score alone, yielding higher recall@k for confirmed remediation actions on held-out weeks.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
Build the experiment around weekly ranking episodes, because that is how vulnerability management is actually run. For each week in an 18-month history, create the candidate set of open asset-vulnerability findings visible to the security team at that time. Join scanner outputs, CMDB attributes, network topology summaries, and remediation system events using only information timestamped on or before the scoring week. Define relevance from downstream operational evidence: a finding is relevant if it is confirmed remediated within 21 days of entering the queue or if it is escalated through incident-response validation in that window. This is not a perfect exploitability label, but it is the best decision-proximal outcome available and is aligned with what the team ultimately acts on.

Use a strict chronological split: train on the earliest 12 months, tune on the next 3 months, and reserve the final 3 months for test. This avoids leakage from future patching behavior, future asset classifications, and evolving network state. Any preprocessing that learns from data distribution, including imputation, scaling, or frequency encoding, is fit on the training window only. Graph features such as exposure counts, vulnerable-neighbor counts, and centrality summaries are recomputed per week from the network data available at that point in time.

Train a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank model with weekly query groups so the model directly learns within-week prioritization. The main comparison should be against the operational baseline of sorting by CVSS score, with deterministic vendor-severity tie-breaks. Also include a stronger non-ranking baseline: logistic regression on the same tabular non-graph signals with comparable hyperparameter tuning effort. That establishes whether any gain comes from the richer ranking formulation and nonlinear interactions rather than from simply adding obvious metadata.

Evaluate primarily with NDCG@100. In this setting, the team reviews only a bounded top slice of the weekly queue, and the practical objective is to concentrate the findings most likely to warrant action near the very top, not merely to improve average ordering over the full tail. NDCG@100 captures both truncation and position sensitivity, which is appropriate for analyst workflows with finite triage bandwidth. Report Recall@100, Recall@50, and MAP as secondary metrics so the result can also be interpreted in terms of coverage of acted-on findings.

Select hyperparameters using mean validation NDCG@100 across weeks, then retrain on train plus validation with the chosen settings and score the untouched test weeks once. To address confounding, run ablations for severity-only, asset-only, and asset-plus-exposure inputs, and report stratified results for internet-exposed versus internal assets and for high- versus lower-criticality systems. This experiment will establish whether combining inventory, topology, and scanner signals improves operational prioritization over CVSS alone on future weeks. It will not establish causal exploitability in the absence of action-label bias, so conclusions should remain about prioritization effectiveness under real enterprise workflow constraints.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model using sequence features from source code changes, pull-request metadata, and developer collaboration graph signals will predict post-merge production incidents better than a baseline trained only on engineered repository-level metrics for enterprise DevOps teams, as measured by out-of-time AUROC and precision at the top risk decile.
domain: Software engineering / DevOps analytics for enterprise code repositories and pull requests
task_prompt:
Use merged pull requests as the prediction unit and construct a 24-month historical dataset from source-control, code-review, CI, deployment, and incident systems for repositories with reliable merge-to-deploy linkage. Define the label as whether the deployed PR is associated with a production incident or rollback within 7 days of first production deployment. Because attribution is imperfect, keep the labeling rule operationally strict and consistent: only incidents tied through deployment or rollback records are positive. Exclude PRs lacking deployment traceability from supervised training, but report how much traffic this removes to quantify coverage bias.

Respect the production setting with a chronological split by merge date: train on the earliest 70% of the timeline, tune on the next 15%, and reserve the final 15% as a pure out-of-time test. This matches the stated goal of forecasting future risky changes under process drift. Leakage is controlled by computing every feature from information available no later than the PR merge timestamp and by enforcing the 7-day outcome window before assigning labels. Encoders, imputers, and any normalization statistics are fit only on training data.

Engineer three feature families. First, sequence features summarize the dynamics of change creation and review: number of commits, elapsed time between commits, churn trajectories, touched-file entropy, reviewer turnaround, comment-thread depth, CI rerun sequences, and failure-to-pass patterns. Second, PR and repository context features capture static and recent operational state: changed files, added/deleted lines, language mix, service ownership, historical deploy cadence, rolling incident rate, branch age, and CI stability. Third, developer collaboration graph features capture organization structure at merge time: author-reviewer centrality, prior collaboration counts, cross-team review edges, reviewer-set novelty, and bus-factor proxies. Exclude any feature generated after merge or derived from incident systems except lagged historical aggregates.

Train a LightGBM classifier on the full feature set with class weighting and early stopping. Tune depth, leaves, learning rate, minimum leaf size, and subsampling on the validation block. Compare it against a fair baseline of the same model family trained only on repository-level engineered metrics. Using the same learner and tuning budget isolates the value of sequence and collaboration signals rather than conflating feature improvements with a more expressive algorithm.

Use AUROC on the out-of-time test set as the primary metric. The deployment use case is score-based triage: engineering leaders need a stable ranking of merged changes so scarce additional review, canarying, or staged rollout capacity can be directed toward the riskiest items across many teams. Since incident prevalence is low, also report precision and recall at the top risk decile, average precision, and Brier score. Model selection is driven by validation AUROC, with a check that top-decile precision is not materially degraded.

Control for confounds by excluding raw repository and team identifiers, using lagged and smoothed historical-rate features, and reporting subgroup performance by repository criticality, business unit, and incident prevalence. The experiment establishes whether richer code-change and collaboration signals improve incident-risk prediction beyond historical repository context under realistic forward-looking evaluation; it does not establish causal mechanisms for why individual changes fail in production.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data will surface the vulnerabilities most likely to be exploited within 30 days better than a severity-only ranking based on CVSS scores.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
To test the hypothesis, build a retrospective ranking dataset from 12 months of vulnerability management operations data. Each example is an asset-vulnerability finding at the time it first appears in a scanner snapshot. Join scanner output to asset inventory, CMDB criticality, exposure metadata, and snapshot-consistent network topology summaries. Define the outcome on a 30-day forward window: a finding is positive if the same vulnerability on the same asset is later confirmed exploited or linked to an incident within 30 days through EDR, SIEM, threat hunting, or incident-response records. Restrict the study to assets with stable telemetry and scanner coverage so negatives are not dominated by simple observability gaps.

Use a chronological split by observation week: 70% train, 15% validation, 15% test. This is the correct deployment-faithful split because ranking decisions are made on newly observed findings, and any random interleaving of weeks would let the model absorb future remediation behavior, incident investigation intensity, or changing scanner policy. All preprocessing is fit on training only. Imputation, categorical encoding, and any scaling are learned on the training period and then applied unchanged to validation and test.

Engineer features from four sources: vulnerability attributes, asset context, finding state, and network topology. Include CVSS components, scanner severity, vulnerability age, exploit-publication flags if available, asset OS and role, business criticality, external exposure, prior patch latency by asset class, local vulnerability density, and graph summaries such as connectivity, segment exposure, and neighborhood risk concentration. Exclude any feature created after ranking time, especially remediation ticket status, analyst annotations, and incident workflow fields.

Train a LambdaMART learning-to-rank model with observation week as the ranking group. This matches the operational problem: the security team reviews a bounded weekly queue and needs the highest-risk findings surfaced first. Compare against a severity-only CVSS ranking, which is the practical incumbent, and against a logistic regression baseline limited to CVSS-derived vulnerability fields so the comparison is not merely “model versus heuristic” but “enterprise context versus severity-only information.” Give both learned models equivalent validation-based tuning effort.

Select hyperparameters on the validation period using mean NDCG@100 across weeks, with early stopping and a modest search over tree depth, learning rate, and leaf size. NDCG@100 is the primary metric because remediation capacity is finite and the operational question is whether the top review set contains the findings most likely to be exploited soon. Also report Precision@25, Recall@100, MAP, and AUCPR for supplementary visibility into extreme-top performance and class imbalance.

Hold the test set untouched until the end, then report final performance with bootstrap confidence intervals over weeks. To address confounds, repeat evaluation within internet-exposed vs internal assets, high- vs lower-criticality assets, and servers vs endpoints, and run feature-family ablations for recency, exposure, and criticality. This experiment will show whether adding enterprise context improves prioritization beyond CVSS-centric ranking under realistic operational constraints.

hypothesis: Re] Graph Edit Networks
task_prompt:
The paper introduces Graph Edit Networks (GENs), a graph neural network architecture designed to predict edits that transform an input graph into a target graph. Rather than directly outputting a full graph, the model predicts local edit operations: node insertions, node deletions, and edge insertions or deletions. The architecture computes node representations with message passing over the input graph and then applies an output layer that assigns edit scores to nodes and node pairs. These scores are interpreted as edit actions, enabling the network to model graph-to-graph transformations in a structured and sparse way.

The methodology formulates graph editing as a supervised learning problem. Training examples consist of input graphs together with desired graph transformations, represented through edit scripts or edit indicators derived from source and target graphs. The model is trained to predict which nodes should be deleted, where new nodes should be inserted, and which edges should be added or removed. The paper evaluates the approach on synthetic graph transformation tasks constructed to reflect rule-based edits, and it compares GENs against alternative neural approaches for graph prediction. Reported experiments examine whether the architecture can learn these transformation rules from examples and generalize to unseen graphs.

The paper also studies a variant with restricted edge prediction to improve efficiency on larger graphs. In this formulation, candidate edge edits are limited using node-level signals so that the model does not need to score all possible node pairs. Experiments are presented to assess both predictive performance on the synthetic tasks and the scalability of the method as graph size increases.

hypothesis: Re] Reproducing 'Identifying through flows for recovering latent representations
task_prompt:
The paper proposes iFlow, a flow-based method for recovering latent representations from observed data. The central experimental goal is to evaluate whether iFlow can more accurately identify the underlying latent variables than the existing iVAE approach. The paper claims superiority both in preserving the geometry of the original source manifold and in recovering latent dimensions that are more strongly correlated with the true generating factors.

The reported methodology uses synthetic data with known latent structure so that latent recovery can be measured directly. Both iFlow and iVAE are trained on these generated observations, and their recovered latent spaces are compared against the ground-truth latent variables. Performance is assessed using metrics that quantify latent-variable correspondence, including mean correlation coefficient (MCC), as well as qualitative and geometric comparisons illustrated in figures.

The experiments appear to rely on the authors' implementation for model training and data generation, with specific hyperparameter settings used to run the iFlow and iVAE comparisons. The paper's claimed conclusion is that iFlow consistently outperforms iVAE on the latent recovery task, achieving better alignment with true latent factors and better preservation of the underlying manifold structure.

hypothesis: Re] Learning to count everything
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a few-shot object counting framework called FamNet that treats counting as a regression problem conditioned on a small number of exemplars from the target category. Given an input image and a few example bounding boxes of the object of interest taken from the same image, the model learns to estimate a density map whose integral gives the final count. This formulation is intended to generalize to novel object categories without category-specific retraining, enabling counting across many visual domains from only a few exemplars at test time.

FamNet is presented together with a test-time adaptation strategy for novel categories. The method uses exemplar patches to condition the counting model and refines prediction for the current category using limited supervision available at inference time. The paper evaluates the effect of the number of exemplars and claims that increasing exemplar count improves accuracy. The approach is compared against prior few-shot counting methods as well as detector-based baselines to show improved counting performance.

To support broad-category evaluation, the paper introduces the FSC-147 dataset, containing more than 6000 images spanning 147 visual categories with annotations suitable for few-shot counting. Experiments are conducted on FSC-147 and also on CARPK, with additional analysis intended to show that the approach remains effective not only on the newly introduced multi-category dataset but also on category-specific counting benchmarks. The main claimed findings are that FamNet outperforms competing few-shot approaches, performs well on category-specific data, and benefits from more exemplars.

hypothesis: Re] Domain Generalization using Causal Matching
task_prompt:
The paper studies domain generalization under the assumption that a model should learn representations that transfer across unseen domains by focusing on stable, causal features of the underlying object rather than domain-specific artifacts. It argues that the commonly used objective of class-conditional domain invariance is insufficient, because samples with the same class label can still differ in their stable causal features across domains. To motivate this claim, the paper presents theoretical and empirical counterexamples showing that conditioning only on class labels does not guarantee the desired invariance.

To address this, the paper proposes a causal matching objective for representation learning. The goal is to learn representations such that inputs originating from the same underlying object-related factors are mapped similarly across domains, even when superficial or domain-specific characteristics change. This approach is intended to capture within-class variation in stable features and produce a classifier that generalizes better to domains not seen during training.

The methodology is evaluated in a standard multi-domain supervised learning setting, where training data come from multiple source domains and performance is tested on unseen target domains. Experiments are conducted on benchmark datasets including Rotated-MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, PACS, and Chest-Xray, comparing the proposed method against prior domain generalization approaches. The paper’s empirical design is meant to test whether causal matching yields more domain-invariant and transferable representations than methods based only on class-conditional invariance.

hypothesis: Re] Reproducibility Study - SCOUTER: Slot Attention-based Classifier for Explainable Image Recognition
task_prompt:
The paper introduces SCOUTER, a slot-attention-based image classifier designed to provide explainable image recognition. The claimed methodology compares SCOUTER against alternative explanation methods and against a fully connected classifier baseline. The evaluation is structured around three main claims: that SCOUTER produces better explanations than competing explanation methods under several explanation-quality metrics, that its classification accuracy remains comparable to a fully connected model, and that it achieves stronger confusion-matrix-based performance on a binary classification task.

To support these claims, the paper trains SCOUTER and comparison models on multiple image datasets and evaluates them in two ways. First, it assesses the quality of generated explanations using a set of explanation evaluation metrics intended to quantify how well the model highlights relevant image regions, including both positive and negative patterns. Second, it compares standard predictive performance measures such as classification accuracy across datasets, and additionally computes confusion-matrix-derived metrics for a binary classification setting.

The proposed experimental design therefore combines interpretability evaluation and predictive performance evaluation. SCOUTER is presented as an architecture that preserves competitive recognition performance while yielding explanations that are more effective than those from existing explanation approaches, with the binary classification experiments used to further argue for favorable class-wise decision behavior relative to a fully connected baseline.

hypothesis: For enterprise email security, a transformer model fine-tuned on message text plus header and URL features will detect spear-phishing emails better than a gradient-boosted baseline using only engineered metadata, measured by PR-AUC on a temporally held-out test set.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
Use 12 months of inbound mail-gateway data from a single enterprise and join each message to the telemetry available at receipt time: raw subject/body text, routing and authentication headers, sender and recipient metadata, URL and attachment indicators, and point-in-time sender/domain/IP reputation logs. Define positives as emails confirmed malicious or spear-phishing by analysts within 14 days of receipt. Define negatives from messages explicitly resolved as benign or bulk/spam and never later escalated. Exclude unlabeled or weakly adjudicated traffic from supervised training so the comparison is driven by reliable outcomes.

Because attacker tactics and local mail policy drift over time, split strictly by receipt timestamp: train on months 1-8, validate on months 9-10, and reserve months 11-12 as a final future-like test. Before splitting, cluster near-duplicate messages and campaign variants using normalized body similarity, shared URL infrastructure, and subject/header overlap, then assign each cluster wholly to one partition based on its first appearance. This prevents the model from seeing one copy of a phishing wave during training and another in test. All transformations are fit on training only, including tokenization decisions, imputers, and categorical encodings.

Train the proposed model by fine-tuning a pretrained transformer on subject and body text, then concatenate the pooled text representation with structured features from headers, URLs, attachments, sender-recipient history, authentication outcomes, and reputation snapshots. Feed the combined representation through a small classification head. Compare against a strong gradient-boosted tree baseline built on engineered metadata only, excluding free text so the experiment directly tests whether message language plus structured context adds detection value beyond conventional gateway features. Give both models comparable tuning effort on the validation window.

Use PR-AUC as the primary metric on the temporally held-out test set. In this setting, analysts consume a ranked queue of suspicious emails rather than a single fixed decision threshold, and the practical objective is to place truly malicious messages as high in that queue as possible despite extreme class imbalance. PR-AUC therefore captures whether the new model improves triage quality across the range of alert volumes the security team can realistically review. To make the result operationally interpretable, also report recall at fixed alert-volume budgets, precision in the top 500 and top 1000 weekly alerts, AUROC, calibration diagnostics, and campaign-level recall after deduplication.

Perform model selection and early stopping only on the validation period, choosing the best checkpoint and hyperparameters by PR-AUC. Touch the test set once at the end. To address confounds, run ablations that remove reputation features and, separately, remove body text so any gain can be attributed more precisely to learned language versus sender cues. Also report slice performance on previously unseen sender domains and previously unseen campaign clusters. This design establishes whether the multimodal transformer gives better future-period phishing ranking than a strong metadata baseline under realistic drift and duplicate-control constraints; it does not by itself prove robustness across organizations with different mail mixes or analyst labeling practices.

hypothesis: Re] Privacy-preserving collaborative learning with automatic transformation search
task_prompt:
The paper proposes ATSPrivacy, a privacy-preserving collaborative learning framework that applies data augmentations to training images before gradient sharing in order to reduce the risk of gradient-based reconstruction attacks. The central idea is that simple image transformations such as translations, contrast changes, and related augmentations can obfuscate private image content while preserving enough task-relevant information for effective model training.

To choose effective transformations, the method defines a policy as a sequence or combination of augmentations and introduces an automatic transformation search procedure. This search is designed to identify policies that maximize privacy protection while maintaining predictive performance. Because directly evaluating every candidate policy through full model training and reconstruction attacks is expensive, the framework uses search metrics including a privacy score and an accuracy score as proxies to guide policy selection.

The methodology evaluates whether the selected augmentation policies improve privacy under a gradient reconstruction threat model while retaining model utility. The paper claims that the searched policies provide stronger protection than using individual augmentations alone, and that the proposed search metrics correlate with downstream privacy and accuracy outcomes, enabling efficient discovery of effective privacy-preserving augmentation policies for collaborative learning.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity threat detection, a graph-based model that incorporates identity, device, and email interaction links will detect account-takeover incidents more accurately than a feature-only gradient-boosted baseline when evaluated on future incident windows.
domain: Cybersecurity threat detection for enterprise account takeover investigation using identity provider logs, device telemetry, and email security events
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis with a time-respecting comparison between a heterogeneous graph model and a strong feature-only gradient-boosted baseline on enterprise account-takeover detection. Start from 12 months of identity provider logs, endpoint telemetry, email security events, and analyst-confirmed investigation outcomes. Define each prediction unit as a user/session or 24-hour alert window, labeled positive when the investigation is closed as account takeover and negative when reviewed and closed as benign or non-ATO. Exclude unresolved cases and any examples whose labels are not available by the supervision cutoff, since delayed security investigations otherwise inject label noise.

Use a chronological split to match deployment on future attack windows: months 1–8 for training, 9–10 for validation, and 11–12 for test. This is necessary because attack tooling, infrastructure, and user behavior drift over time. To limit campaign leakage, keep exact case or campaign identifiers inside one split and remove features tied to analyst workflow timing or case closure state. All imputers, category thresholds, normalization parameters, and any learned preprocessing are fit on training only and then frozen.

For the baseline, build a tabular feature set from signals available at scoring time: login failure and MFA patterns, impossible-travel indicators, first-seen device/IP flags, device health and posture, sender novelty, burstiness of inbound email, mailbox rule changes, and historical user risk aggregates. For the graph model, use the same node attributes but add typed edges capturing user-device authentications, user-IP associations, user-sender contacts, and device-email relationships, with recency and interaction-count weights. Construct graphs using only data available up to the relevant split boundary so future edges never enter training or inference.

Train a heterogeneous graph neural network with message passing across user, device, IP, and sender nodes and a binary classification head. Compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline tuned with the same discipline: validation-based search over learning rate, depth, number of trees, sampling parameters, and class weighting. This keeps the experiment focused on whether relational structure adds predictive value beyond strong hand-engineered aggregates.

Select models on the validation window using average precision as the primary metric. In this setting the analyst team triages a ranked queue under heavy class imbalance, so concentrating true takeovers near the top of the score distribution is the most useful behavior. Also report recall at fixed precision levels, precision in the top 100 and top 500 alerts per week, AUROC, and calibration diagnostics. The final 2-month test window is touched once after model family and hyperparameters are locked, and performance differences are summarized with bootstrap confidence intervals.

To address confounds, run ablations removing raw high-cardinality identifiers while preserving behavioral summaries, and report results separately for previously seen versus newly observed users and devices. This experiment will establish whether relational modeling improves future-window detection under realistic drift and label delay; it will not by itself prove that every observed gain comes from abstract graph reasoning rather than specific recurrent entity patterns.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer model that combines inbound message text, header features, URL features, and sender-reputation signals will detect phishing emails more accurately than a feature-limited baseline using only metadata and handcrafted rules, especially on novel campaign variants seen in a later time period.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on a 12-month stream of real inbound email telemetry captured at delivery time. The training corpus should include subject and body text, parsed headers, authentication results, URL and attachment indicators, and sender/domain reputation signals that were actually available when the message arrived. Labels should come from confirmed phishing verdicts established through analyst review, incident response, or validated user reports. Because some cases are resolved late, use a 30-day label maturation window and drop unresolved messages rather than forcing noisy negatives. This keeps supervision cleaner while still reflecting realistic class imbalance.

The split should be strictly chronological: months 1-8 for training, 9-10 for validation, and 11-12 for test. That is the right design for this domain because phishing tactics, domains, and message templates drift over time, and a later-period test is the only credible way to assess detection of novel campaigns. To further limit contamination, remove exact duplicates and near-duplicate campaign variants that would otherwise appear across windows through replayed templates or shared URL kits.

Feature construction should mirror what the gateway can know before delivery. For text, encode subject plus normalized body content. For structured inputs, include sender/domain age, authentication pass/fail, header inconsistencies, display-name deception indicators, URL lexical and hosting features, attachment type features, and historical sender aggregates computed only from events prior to the message timestamp. Fit all preprocessing objects—tokenizer, vocabularies, imputers, scalers, and any frequency encoders—on training data only, then freeze them for validation and test. Explicitly exclude analyst verdict fields, case metadata, quarantine actions, and any post-delivery user interaction signals because they can encode the label.

Train a multimodal transformer that produces a text representation from subject/body and fuses it with structured features through a classification head. The fair baseline is not a weak rules engine alone; it should be a competitive metadata-only system, such as gradient-boosted trees over headers, authentication, URL, attachment, and handcrafted rule outputs, tuned with the same validation window and similar search effort. That comparison isolates whether adding content and multimodal modeling improves detection beyond mature metadata-driven defenses.

Use partial AUROC in the region up to 0.5% false-positive rate as the primary metric. In this environment, analysts and gateway policies act on the highest-risk slice of mail, so model quality is determined by how well it orders malicious messages above legitimate mail where only a very small false-positive budget is tolerable. Report recall at 0.1% and 0.5% FPR, PR-AUC, full ROC-AUC, and calibration diagnostics as secondary views.

Select hyperparameters and early stopping based on the validation window only; touch the test set once at the end. In addition to overall performance, report results on held-out campaign clusters, business units, and language strata. This experiment would establish whether multimodal transformers improve phishing detection under realistic temporal drift and low-FPR operating conditions. It would not by itself establish robustness to future adversarial adaptation beyond the observed test period or guarantee transportability to organizations with materially different mail distributions.

hypothesis: Re] Learning Unknown from Correlations: Graph Neural Network for
task_prompt:
The paper studies multi-type protein-protein interaction prediction with a focus on interactions between proteins that are both unseen during training, referred to as inter-novel-protein interactions. It argues that standard random train/test splits can obscure this setting and therefore proposes a new evaluation framework designed to better reflect generalization to novel proteins. The evaluation uses multiple train/test construction strategies, including random splits and graph-based splitting schemes such as breadth-first-search (BFS) and depth-first-search (DFS), to control how proteins and their interactions are distributed between training and test sets. Performance is assessed both overall and specifically on inter-novel-protein interactions, with micro-F1 used as a main metric.

To address the prediction task itself, the paper introduces GNN-PPI, a graph neural network method that leverages correlations between proteins through a protein interaction graph. The method is intended to improve prediction quality for interactions involving proteins not observed during training by incorporating relational structure beyond sequence-only modeling. The proposed approach is compared against prior protein-protein interaction methods, including conventional machine learning baselines and deep learning baselines such as PIPR, DNN-PPI, and DPPI.

Experiments are reported on several real-world datasets of different scales, including SHS27k, SHS148k, and STRING. The paper claims that GNN-PPI achieves higher micro-F1 than strong baselines, especially PIPR, across the proposed split strategies, and that its gains are particularly pronounced for inter-novel-protein interaction prediction. It also claims that the proposed evaluation framework provides a more consistent and realistic assessment of cross-dataset generalization and prediction performance for newly discovered proteins.

hypothesis: Re] Robust Counterfactual Explanations on Graph Neural Networks
task_prompt:
The paper proposes RCExplainer, a method for generating counterfactual explanations for graph neural networks in graph classification tasks. In this setting, an explanation is represented as a subset of edges from the input graph. The method is designed so that removing the selected edges leads to a substantial change in the model's prediction, making the explanation counterfactual. The paper positions this as an alternative to prior GNN explanation methods that aim to identify important substructures but do not explicitly optimize for counterfactual behavior or robustness.

The experimental methodology compares RCExplainer against existing GNN explanation baselines on graph classification benchmarks. Explanations are evaluated along three axes: counterfactual quality as measured by fidelity-style metrics based on the effect of removing explanation edges, robustness to noise or perturbations, and computational efficiency. The paper reports training the underlying GNNs and the explainer models, then evaluating the generated edge subsets under these metrics to assess whether RCExplainer produces more effective and stable explanations than prior methods.

The central claim tested by these experiments is that RCExplainer outperforms competing explainers by generating explanations that are more counterfactual, more robust to noisy conditions, and at least as efficient in runtime. The methodology therefore consists of training the explainer, applying it to graph classification models, and benchmarking its explanations against alternative explainers using the same evaluation framework.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that combines SIEM alert features with incident response ticket text and historical analyst feedback will surface true high-priority incidents above the fold more effectively than a classifier-based alert scoring baseline trained only on structured alert metadata.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on production SOC alert streams joined to downstream incident response records over an 18-month period. The unit of prediction is the alert at triage time, but training and evaluation are done as grouped ranking problems so the model learns which alerts should rise within the set actually competing for analyst attention. To keep the study aligned with deployment, include only production alert types and queues that feed the analyst workflow you intend to support.

Labels should reflect downstream operational consequence rather than raw analyst queue order. A practical graded relevance target is 2 for alerts tied to confirmed High/Critical incidents, malicious findings, or containment/remediation within 7 days; 1 for confirmed Medium-severity incidents; and 0 for benign, duplicate, or non-actionable alerts. Cases unresolved after 7 days should be dropped from supervised training because they do not provide stable supervision. This gives a ranking target that prioritizes the incidents the SOC most wants surfaced immediately.

Because attacker behavior, rule tuning, and asset inventory shift over time, use a strict chronological split: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. Build ranking groups within analyst queue-day and business unit so each query approximates a real triage slate. All transforms are fit on the training window only. Feature generation must be time-safe: numerical aggregates are computed from history strictly before the alert timestamp, and any text used from tickets or case summaries is limited to text available within two hours of alert creation. Final disposition fields, closure codes, post-incident writeups, and explicit queue-order signals are excluded because they would leak outcomes or simply reproduce prior human decisions.

The proposed model is LambdaMART, using structured SIEM/EDR metadata, short-horizon alert history, entity recurrence statistics, asset/user context, MITRE mappings, and sparse text features from early ticket content. Historical analyst feedback is included only as prior-pattern summaries for rule-asset-user combinations, never as the current alert's own outcome fields. The main baseline is a gradient-boosted classifier trained only on structured metadata and ranked by predicted probability within the same queue groups. This baseline is the correct comparison for the hypothesis because it isolates both the ranking formulation and the added information source from a realistic metadata-only scoring system.

Use NDCG@10 as the primary metric, with MRR, Recall@10, Precision@10, and AUCPR on a binary severe-incident label as secondary metrics. Model selection is performed on the validation window using NDCG@10 with grouped bootstrap intervals over queue-days, and the test window is held untouched until the end. Finally, run ablations by feature family and subgroup checks by business unit, alert family, and unseen rule IDs to determine whether gains are broad or concentrated. The experiment can establish improved historical triage prioritization under drift-aware evaluation; it does not by itself prove causal reduction in dwell time or immunity to policy-driven label bias.

hypothesis: Re] Does Self-Supervision Always Improve Few-Shot Learning?
task_prompt:
The paper studies whether self-supervised learning can be used as an auxiliary training signal to improve meta-learning-based few-shot classification. The claimed approach augments standard few-shot learners with self-supervised objectives such as rotation prediction or jigsaw prediction, trained jointly without requiring additional labeled data. The goal is to learn feature representations on base classes that transfer better to novel classes in few-shot episodes.

The experimental design compares few-shot learners trained with supervised objectives alone against versions regularized by auxiliary self-supervised tasks. The paper evaluates these methods across several benchmark datasets and domains, and examines whether the benefit of self-supervision depends on task difficulty. In particular, it claims larger gains when supervision is more limited and when images are noisier or lower resolution.

The paper also studies the use of extra unlabeled images from outside the training domain for self-supervised learning. It claims that unlabeled data from dissimilar domains can harm few-shot performance, and proposes a domain selection algorithm to address this. The algorithm is intended to select images from a large generic unlabeled pool that are most similar to the training domain, so that the selected data can be used for self-supervision and improve downstream few-shot accuracy.

hypothesis: Re] FOCUS: Flexible Optimizable Counterfactual Explanations for Tree Ensembles
task_prompt:
The paper proposes FOCUS, a method for generating counterfactual explanations for tree-ensemble classifiers. Given an input instance and a desired target prediction, the method searches for a modified version of the instance that changes the model output while staying close to the original input under a chosen distance function. The approach is designed to be flexible, allowing different optimization objectives and constraints to be incorporated, and is intended to work across tree-based models such as random forests and boosted trees.

Methodologically, the paper formulates counterfactual generation as an optimization problem over the feature space induced by the tree ensemble. It uses the structure of decision trees to identify feasible regions associated with alternative predictions, and then optimizes within those regions to obtain counterfactuals that satisfy the target class while minimizing distance from the original instance. The method is presented as capable of always returning a valid counterfactual explanation for instances in the evaluated datasets.

For evaluation, the paper compares FOCUS against an existing counterfactual explanation method for tree-based models. Experiments are conducted on several datasets and tree-based classifiers, and performance is assessed primarily by whether a valid counterfactual can be found for each instance and by the mean distance between the original input and the generated counterfactual. The paper’s central claim is that FOCUS can generate counterfactual explanations for all tested instances and often finds explanations that are closer to the original input than the baseline method.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection, a sequence model trained on raw endpoint, VPN, and SSO event timelines will detect confirmed malicious incidents with higher AUROC and lower false-positive rate at the same recall than a model built only from aggregated daily counts and hand-engineered user/session features.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection using network flow logs and endpoint/network telemetry from a large corporate environment
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Aggregated Features for Enterprise Intrusion Detection

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a sequence model trained on raw, timestamped endpoint and network event timelines outperforms a traditional feature-engineered baseline (aggregated daily counts and hand-crafted indicators) at detecting confirmed malicious incidents. Success is measured by higher AUROC and reduced false-positive rate at equivalent recall on a held-out test set.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling
We source 18–24 months of production logs from the organization's SOC, including endpoint telemetry, VPN authentication, SSO sign-in records, and process/network events. All timestamps are preserved; no contemporaneous real-time data is included. Ground-truth labels are obtained from SOC investigation records: a confirmed incident is flagged when the investigation team documents evidence of compromise. To account for pre-incident reconnaissance, we label a 72-hour window preceding each incident confirmation as positive. Incidents separated by >30 days on the same user are treated as independent. Benign windows are all remaining user-device-day blocks outside incident windows. This labeling strategy respects the temporal nature of investigations while capturing the early-warning lead time that the SOC values.

### 3. Train/Validation/Test Split
The dataset is split chronologically to prevent future information leakage and to reflect deployment reality. Months 1–12 serve as the training set, months 13–15 as validation, and months 16–18 as test. Within each split, confirmed incidents are stratified to preserve the proportion of incident-bearing user cohorts across splits. This temporal partitioning ensures the test set represents truly future, unseen incidents and precludes any forward-looking bias in model selection.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
All scaling, encoding, and imputation parameters are fit on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

**Sequence model features:** Raw event tuples (timestamp, event_type, source_ip, destination_ip, process_name, user_id, device_id, authentication_status) are extracted and ordered chronologically. Each tuple is embedded: event_type and process_name via learned embeddings; IPs via hashing and embedding; user/device IDs via learned embeddings. A sliding 48-hour observation window is extracted for each prediction point. Events are zero-padded to 500 slots so the model can learn on variable-length sequences. Both groups receive shared metadata features: historical user risk score, device risk score, and a binary indicator of prior incidents.

**Baseline features:** Aggregated daily summaries—failed login counts, VPN connection counts, process execution counts, IP address entropy, device-switching frequency—plus hand-engineered indicators such as off-hours access, impossible travel detection, and repeated failed MFA attempts. These are the features in current operational use and represent the fair baseline.

### 5. Model Selection and Training
**Sequence model:** A 2-layer bidirectional LSTM (128 hidden units per layer) with multi-head attention (4 heads, 64 dimensions) over the event sequence, followed by a 2-layer MLP head (64, 32 units, ReLU, dropout p=0.3) that outputs a binary classification logit.

**Baseline model:** XGBoost, tuned over a 12-point grid (max_depth ∈ [4, 8], learning_rate ∈ [0.01, 0.1], num_estimators ∈ [100, 500]).

Both models are trained on the training set with class-weighted cross-entropy loss (weights inversely proportional to class frequency). Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using AUROC as the selection criterion. No tuning or model modification occurs after test evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Strategy
**Primary metric: AUROC on the test set.** AUROC is appropriate for imbalanced data, is threshold-agnostic, and directly supports the SOC's goal of ranking incidents higher than benign activity so analysts can set their own alert threshold.

**Secondary metrics:**
- False Positive Rate at 90% Recall: fraction of benign events flagged when the model achieves 90% incident recall.
- Lead time to detection: median hours between first model alert and formal incident confirmation.
- Incident Detection Rate at fixed 5% False Positive Rate: reflects practical analyst capacity.
- Precision-Recall AUC: complements AUROC for imbalanced data.

### 7. Controls for Confounds
**Confound: Raw event access vs. temporal modeling.** We control this by training an additional ablation baseline: a dense baseline using raw events as input but with non-sequential featurization (bag-of-events). Comparing sequence model vs. dense baseline isolates the contribution of sequential structure.

**Confound: Temporal distribution shift.** We report incident type proportions and data quality metrics (missingness rates) for each split. Any split exceeding 10% missingness is flagged; results are reported both with and without high-missingness cohorts.

**Test set integrity:** The test set is evaluated exactly once, after model selection is complete. No threshold adjustment or re-tuning occurs post-evaluation.

### 8. Expected Outcome and Scope
This design will establish whether sequence models, trained on raw event timelines, achieve higher AUROC and lower false positives than aggregated-feature baselines when evaluated on future, temporally held-out incidents. It will not establish whether the gains are causal to sequence modeling itself (the dense baseline ablation addresses this) or whether the approach generalizes to different organizations or incident types (out of scope for a single-site evaluation).

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity incident triage, a temporal graph neural network that combines identity, endpoint, and email interaction events over the prior 30 days will detect true malicious incidents with higher PR-AUC than a gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated tabular alert features alone.
domain: Cybersecurity incident detection for enterprise identity, endpoint, and email security operations
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN for Cybersecurity Incident Triage

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of production data from a single enterprise SOC, spanning months 1–18 continuously. The dataset comprises SIEM/SOAR logs, authentication events, endpoint detections, and email security alerts, each with a timestamp and alert properties (severity, source, type). Labels are derived from analyst disposition decisions recorded within 7 days of each alert: confirmed malicious (label=1) or benign/no action (label=0). Alerts without a final disposition after 7 days are excluded to minimize label noise. This is operationally realistic: SOC analysts already perform this triage, and their decisions serve as ground truth.

### 2. Split Strategy

A chronological (temporal) split is mandatory to prevent leakage of future information. The training set comprises alerts from months 1–12, the validation set from months 13–15, and the held-out test set from months 16–18. This split is applied at the alert level (alert timestamp determines assignment). The temporal boundary ensures that the model cannot learn patterns from analyst responses, follow-up detections, or incident context that occur after the alert in question. This reflects deployment: in production, the model scores alerts as they arrive and must predict their disposition using only prior history.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For each alert, a 30-day lookback window is extracted, capturing all events (authentication, endpoint detection, email security) from that entity (user or host) in the preceding 30 days.

**Baseline features (tabular):** Event counts by type, unique entities (users, hosts, domains), time since last event of each type, alert severity, source queue, and business unit. These are aggregated statistics that summarize entity behavior without explicit relationship structure. All scaling is fit on the training set and applied frozen to validation and test.

**GNN features (temporal graph):** A heterogeneous graph is constructed for each alert's 30-day window with three node types—user, host, email_domain—and four edge types: authentication event, endpoint detection, email send, email receive. Each edge is timestamped and carries metadata (severity, protocol, detection name). No post-alert information (analyst actions, subsequent incidents) is included. The graph has an alert node linked to the primary user/host involved; the GNN must predict whether this alert represents a true intrusion based on the surrounding 30-day entity graph.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** A temporal graph neural network with heterogeneous message passing, temporal attention over edge timestamps, and pooling to produce an alert-level score. Hyperparameters include depth (2–4 GNN layers), attention heads, hidden dimension, dropout, temporal decay, and learning rate.

**Baseline:** XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the tabular feature set over the same 30-day window. This baseline is fair because it receives equivalent temporal lookback and entity information; the comparison isolates the value of graph structure. Additionally, a secondary baseline—a Transformer or LSTM that encodes event sequences chronologically—is trained to control for the possibility that the tGNN's advantage comes from temporal modeling rather than graph structure. All baselines receive matched hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search or Bayesian optimization).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) on the test set. This is chosen because incidents are rare (~1–5% of alerts), making ROC-AUC uninformative. PR-AUC reflects the operational goal: rank alerts so analysts see true incidents first, minimizing wasted effort on false alarms. A higher PR-AUC means the model's top-ranked alerts contain more genuine intrusions.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision at fixed recall thresholds (P@R=0.8, P@R=0.9) to quantify analyst labor reduction; recall at acceptable false positive rates (FP@FPR=0.05); calibration metrics (expected calibration error) to ensure predicted scores reflect true probability, enabling downstream threshold tuning by operations teams.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned using the validation set (months 13–15). A grid search or Bayesian optimization iterates over candidate configurations, training on the training set and evaluating on validation data using PR-AUC. The best configuration is selected, then the model is retrained on training + validation data (months 1–15) to maximize use of available non-test data. This final model is evaluated once on the locked test set (months 16–18).

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Three confounds are addressed: (1) *Recency information:* Both models use the same 30-day window, so any tGNN advantage is not due to more recent data alone. (2) *Entity coverage:* Both see the same entity types; a transformer sequence baseline provides additional control that the tGNN's gain is from graph structure, not temporal encoding. (3) *Label noise and analyst drift:* Stratified post-hoc analysis on test subsets (by month, incident type, or analyst) checks whether performance is stable over time. These controls establish that the tGNN's improvement, if found, comes from exploiting relational structure in entity interactions, not from confounding factors.

hypothesis: For hospital readmission risk prediction, a sequence model trained on longitudinal EHR events and lab trajectories will outperform a baseline using only the most recent encounter’s tabular features in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission.
domain: Healthcare analytics in a multi-hospital EHR system, using inpatient and outpatient records with longitudinal patient histories
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We test whether a sequence model trained on longitudinal EHR events (labs, medications, diagnoses, procedures, vitals) outperforms a strong baseline using only the most recent encounter's tabular features in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission. Success means the sequence model achieves meaningfully higher discrimination (AUROC ≥ 0.03 improvement) and maintains clinical calibration at the top-risk tier (PPV ≥ 0.40 among top 10% flagged patients).

### 2. Data Specification
We source 36 months of de-identified EHR data (Jan 2021–Dec 2023) from a multi-hospital network, including all inpatient admissions with complete discharge summaries and linkable outpatient records. Admissions are excluded if: discharge disposition is missing, patient age <18, or the admission is a within-network transfer (keep only the first admission per episode). We audit data completeness: labs ≥80% present in the 12 months prior, medications ≥90%, diagnoses/procedures ≥95%. Records with insufficient history or follow-up status unknown are removed.

The outcome—unplanned 30-day readmission—is defined as any acute inpatient admission within 30 calendar days of discharge, excluding: (1) planned procedures, (2) deaths within 30 days, (3) hospice/palliative discharges. Readmissions outside the network are identified via state hospital discharge databases if available; patients lost to follow-up are excluded (not treated as negative cases).

### 3. Train–Validation–Test Split
We employ a patient-level temporal split to prevent leakage and respect causality:
- **Training (Jan 2021–Aug 2022):** All admissions from patients with ≥1 prior year of history. ~60% of data.
- **Validation (Sep–Dec 2022):** 20% of training patient IDs (random, stratified by index admission risk decile). ~10% of data.
- **Test (Jan–Dec 2023):** All admissions from held-out patient IDs not in training or validation. ~30% of data.

This temporal ordering ensures no future information leaks into features, prevents the same patient from appearing in train and test (reducing optimistic bias), and simulates realistic deployment on new patients.

### 4. Features and Preprocessing
Features are measured over a fixed observation window: [admission_date - 365 days, admission_date), preventing any post-admission data from entering the model.

**Sequence model features:** Chronologically ordered events within 365 days prior: lab results (value, date, clinical flag: normal/low/high), medication fills (drug class, date), diagnoses (ICD-10 grouped to CCS, date), procedures (CPT grouped, date), vitals (BP, HR, temperature, date). Static features: age, sex, race/ethnicity (for fairness monitoring), Charlson comorbidity index (prior year diagnoses).

**Baseline features:** Most recent lab panel (≤14 days prior), most recent vitals (≤7 days prior), active medications at admission, Charlson index, age, sex, count of hospitalizations (30/90 days prior), length of prior stay.

All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only. Categorical vocabularies (diagnoses, procedures, drugs) are defined on training; rare categories in validation/test are binned as 'other.' Missing numeric values are forward-filled within patient history or imputed as training set mean.

### 5. Model and Baseline
**Sequence Model:** Bidirectional LSTM or transformer encoder over variable-length event sequences, with attention pooling to a representation vector, followed by dense layers (128, 64 units, dropout 0.3) and sigmoid output. Hyperparameters: embedding dimension 64, LSTM hidden 128. Tuned on validation set via random search (learning rate, dropout, max sequence length).

**Baseline (Primary Comparison):** Logistic regression on tabular features, same preprocessing, optimized with Adam and early stopping on validation AUROC. A secondary competitor baseline is an XGBoost model on the same tabular feature set, tuned to equivalent effort. Both baselines are strong: they capture recent clinical state and utilization signals. If the sequence model does not substantially outperform them, the hypothesis is not supported.

### 6. Evaluation Strategy
**Primary Metric:** AUROC on the held-out test set. AUROC is threshold-independent and reflects ranking ability, essential for risk stratification. Clinically, we also report sensitivity and PPV at the 90th percentile predicted risk threshold; a useful model must achieve PPV ≥ 0.40 (at least 40% of top-10%-flagged patients are true readmissions).

**Secondary Metrics:** Precision-Recall AUC (for imbalanced data), sensitivity at additional thresholds (95th percentile), calibration (Platt slope and intercept fit on validation, evaluated on test), hospital-stratified AUROC (site-level confounding check), fairness metrics by race/ethnicity and sex.

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set
Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set via random search over 30 configurations. The configuration with the highest validation AUROC is selected. The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once, at the end, under the chosen model.

### 8. Confound Control
**Utilization Intensity:** A critic may argue the sequence model merely captures admission frequency or length-of-stay. To control for this, the baseline model includes explicit utilization counts and prior stay length. If the sequence model does not beat this utilization-aware baseline, the gain is not due to temporal structure. Additionally, we compute attention weights on test data; if attention concentrates on utilization features, the model is not learning temporal dynamics. Stratified analysis by utilization group confirms added value.

**Censoring and Lost Follow-up:** Patients lost to follow-up are excluded (not classified as non-readmissions), preventing bias from network boundaries. Sensitivity analysis re-runs with excluded patients as conservative negatives.

**Hospital Coding Differences:** Stratified evaluation by hospital site checks for confounding by site-level coding practices. Sites with AUROC variation >0.1 are investigated for code-quality issues.

### 9. Success Criteria
The hypothesis is supported if: (1) sequence model AUROC ≥ test baseline AUROC + 0.03, (2) PPV ≥ 0.40 at 90th percentile threshold, (3) calibration slope within [0.8, 1.2] on test set, (4) stratified analysis shows gains across utilization groups, not driven by single site.

hypothesis: Re] Reproducing Learning to Deceive With Attention-Based Explanations
task_prompt:
The paper studies whether attention weights can be relied on as explanations of model decisions by introducing user-defined impermissible tokens into NLP tasks. These tokens represent features that should not be used as explanations, such as protected-trait words or other bias-inducing terms. The core experimental design compares model behavior when such tokens are available, removed, or discouraged through training objectives, with the goal of testing whether a model can continue to use impermissible information while appearing not to attend to it.

For classification tasks, the paper evaluates several architectures including embedding-based models, BiLSTMs, and BERT-style models on datasets such as occupation prediction, gender identity, and SST augmented with wiki-based impermissible tokens. The methodology first measures performance when impermissible tokens are removed from the input, using any resulting drop in accuracy as evidence that the model had been relying on those tokens. It then trains models with an additional penalty term that discourages attention to impermissible tokens while leaving those tokens present in the input, and compares predictive performance and attention distributions to the unpenalized setting.

For sequence-to-sequence tasks, the paper applies the same idea to synthetic and translation datasets including Bigram Flip, Sequence Copy, Sequence Reverse, and English-German translation. The training objective penalizes attention assigned to impermissible tokens, and the evaluation checks whether task performance is preserved even when attention to those tokens is suppressed. The paper argues that if models retain performance despite low visible attention on impermissible tokens, then attention weights can be manipulated and may fail to faithfully explain which input information the model is using.

hypothesis: A transformer-based semantic search model trained on query–document pairs from an enterprise support knowledge base will retrieve relevant help articles more accurately than a BM25 baseline for resolving customer tickets, measured on held-out future tickets.
domain: Enterprise customer support knowledge base search for a SaaS product
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Semantic Search for Support Tickets (hyp_071)

### Objective
Determine whether a transformer-based semantic search model retrieves relevant KB articles more accurately than BM25 for resolving customer support tickets, tested on held-out future data under realistic conditions.

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18–24 months of production data from the SaaS company's support system, comprising:
- All KB articles available during the period (timestamped creation and updates)
- Support tickets (timestamped submissions, agent search queries with clicked article IDs)
- Resolution metadata (time-to-resolution, agent satisfaction if available)

**Ground-truth construction:** Implicit labels from agent behavior are inherently noisy. To build robust ground truth, a stratified sample of ~500–1000 query–article pairs is selected uniformly across time periods and product versions, intentionally oversampling low-click articles to avoid popularity bias. Three independent human annotators label each pair; pairs where annotators agree (2–3 votes) are retained as true relevance labels. Borderline cases and disagreements are excluded. This hybrid approach—anchored on explicit human judgment but validated on implicit signals—reduces noise while maintaining scale.

### 2. Temporal Train-Validation-Test Split

Because the task involves real-world drift (evolving product versions, terminology changes, new issue types), a **temporal split is mandatory**:
- **Training:** Months 1–12 (oldest data)
- **Validation:** Months 13–15 (middle period)
- **Test:** Months 16–18 (most recent, held-out, touched only once)

This ordering respects causality: the model is not exposed to future queries or articles during training. A random split would artificially inflate performance by allowing the model to learn patterns from temporally nearby test examples, violating the premise that agents encounter genuinely new patterns over time.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Features for the transformer:**
- Query text (ticket subject + agent search query, tokenized via subword encoding)
- Document text (KB article title + body, tokenized identically)
- Document metadata: article age, recency of last edit, product version tags, category (one-hot)

**Features for BM25:** Same raw texts, no metadata.

**Fit-apply boundary:** All tokenizers, vocabularies, and scaling parameters are fit **only on the training set**. These fitted transformers are applied without retraining to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage: the model cannot implicitly learn from validation/test vocabulary patterns.

### 4. Baseline and Model Selection

**Baseline:** Okapi BM25, hyperparameter-tuned on the validation set via grid search (k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0], b ∈ [0.5, 0.75]). BM25 is given the same preprocessing and tuning effort as the transformer.

**Model:** A transformer-based dual-encoder (e.g., fine-tuned BERT or DPR). Pre-trained on a general corpus, then fine-tuned on training-set labeled pairs using in-batch negatives and hard negative mining. Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, margin, temperature) are tuned on the validation set. The checkpoint achieving the highest validation Recall@10 is selected.

**Selection criterion:** Recall@10 on the validation set. Model selection does not touch the test set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Recall@10** on the test set. Justification: Support agents typically scan the top 5–15 results; Recall@10 directly measures whether at least one relevant article appears in that window. This aligns with the operational goal of faster first response.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall@5 (stricter, captures tighter ranking)
- nDCG@10 (incorporates ranking quality)
- Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR; measures how soon the first relevant article appears)
- Coverage (fraction of queries with at least one relevant result)

### 6. Confound Controls and Stratified Analysis

To distinguish genuine semantic improvement from confounds:

1. **Temporal stratification:** Report Recall@10 separately for test queries from early product versions vs. recent versions. If the transformer outperforms only on recent data, drift handling may explain gains.

2. **Click-volume analysis:** Evaluate both models on articles stratified by historical click quartile (low-click, high-quality articles vs. popular articles). A gap here indicates exposure bias in training data, not semantic superiority.

3. **Rare-term audit:** Identify queries with rare terms; measure whether the transformer's advantage concentrates on these. This flags whether improvement is driven by better subword handling rather than semantics.

4. **Category and version stratification:** Report performance breakdowns by KB category and product version to detect domain-specific or temporal confounds.

5. **Qualitative spot-check:** Manually inspect top-ranked results for a sample of test queries to identify whether gains reflect semantic reasoning or are artifacts of label noise or data characteristics.

### 7. Test Set Policy

The test set is sealed until model selection is complete. A single evaluation pass is conducted after hyperparameter tuning concludes. All reported metrics—Recall@10, Recall@5, nDCG@10, MRR, and coverage—come from this one test-set evaluation. No threshold tuning or re-ranking occurs based on test results.

### 8. Claims and Scope

This experiment establishes that, **given the labeled test data and evaluation protocol, the transformer retrieves relevant articles with higher recall than BM25 on held-out future tickets.** It does not isolate *why* (semantic understanding vs. other factors) without post-hoc analysis. Stratified results and qualitative audits inform interpretation but do not prove causation.

hypothesis: For a large online learning platform, a time-aware transformer model trained on the full sequence of student activity events over a course term will predict final course completion more accurately than a feature-based gradient-boosted baseline built from aggregate counts and recency features, when both are evaluated on future cohorts.
domain: Online education platform learner behavior modeling using clickstream, assignment, forum, and quiz interaction logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Time-Aware Transformer for Course Completion Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses event-level interaction logs spanning 8 consecutive academic terms (≈24 months) from the platform. Each record comprises a student ID, course ID, instructor ID, term ID, event type (page view, video watch, quiz attempt, assignment submission, forum post), precise timestamp, and associated metadata (quiz score, assignment score, etc.). The outcome label is binary: completion status (pass/completion = 1; non-completion or failure = 0) extracted from official registrar records at term end. Ambiguous cases (administrative withdrawal, incomplete records) are resolved using a pre-agreed categorization rule documented before training begins. This ensures that the label reflects genuine course outcomes, not platform heuristics, and that all models see the same ground truth.

### 2. Train/Validation/Test Split and Prevention of Leakage

Data are split chronologically and by cohort: Terms 1–5 (≈15 months) form the training set. Terms 6–7 (≈6 months) form the validation set. Term 8 (≈3 months) is the held-out test set. This temporal ordering is essential because it mirrors deployment: we train on historical cohorts, validate on recent-past cohorts, and test on a held-out prospective cohort. Any event that occurs after a student's course ends (e.g., final grades, post-course reflections) is excluded. Predictions are made at a fixed point in time—the midterm or 6-week mark—using only events that have occurred up to that point. No information about events after this prediction point is available to either model. This design prevents the most common leakage risk in learner modeling: using late-term behavior to predict early-term outcomes.

The temporal split also exposes distribution shift. Student behavior and course offerings may evolve across terms; evaluating on term 8 after training on terms 1–5 measures generalization to a new cohort in a new term, not memorization of a fixed dataset.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
