hypothesis: Re] Replication study of 'Data-Driven Methods for Balancing Fairness and Efficiency in Ride-Pooling
task_prompt:
The paper studies how different optimization objectives in ride-pooling affect efficiency and fairness for both riders and drivers. It evaluates four objective functions within a dispatching framework based on a Markov decision process combined with a neural value estimator, following a non-myopic matching approach that considers future consequences of current assignments. The system operates on a strongly connected street-network graph with precomputed routes and travel times between all node pairs, and uses trip-request data in which each request has an origin, destination, and request time.

The methodology compares two profitability-oriented objectives and two fairness-oriented objectives. The profitability objectives are: maximizing the total number of serviced requests, and maximizing total driver income. The rider-fairness objective seeks to maintain profitability while reducing disparities in service rates across neighborhoods, and the driver-fairness objective seeks to reduce inequality in income across drivers while still accounting for system performance. For each objective, the dispatching algorithm is run in simulation on New York Yellow Taxi request data to produce ride matches over time.

The paper evaluates each objective using three main outcomes: overall success rate, defined as the percentage of all requests that are serviced; success rate in the worst-served neighborhood, used as a measure of rider fairness; and the distribution of income across drivers, including attention to low-earning drivers as a measure of driver fairness. The overall success rate is treated as a proxy for company profitability. The paper analyzes the trade-offs among these metrics across the four objectives, including whether fairness-oriented objectives can improve worst-neighborhood service or driver income equity without sacrificing overall system efficiency.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that uses the full clinical note text from the first 24 hours of ICU admission will predict in-hospital sepsis onset better than a baseline model using only structured vitals and labs, measured on future admissions from the same hospital system.
domain: Hospital ICU sepsis prediction using electronic health records and early progress notes
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Sepsis Prediction with Clinical Notes

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

This experiment uses retrospective ICU electronic health records spanning 36 months (January 2021 through December 2023) from a single hospital system. The study population includes all ICU admissions with complete vital signs, laboratory results, and clinical progress notes recorded within the first 24 hours of ICU entry. Admissions are excluded if sepsis is documented in the first 6 hours (presumed present-on-admission), if critical data are missing, or if progress notes contain fewer than 50 tokens (indicating minimal or template-only documentation).

Sepsis onset is defined using a composite criterion: the first moment at which an infection marker (positive culture, procalcitonin >2 ng/mL, or infection-related diagnosis code) **and** organ dysfunction (elevated lactate, acute kidney injury, thrombocytopenia, or vasopressor-dependent hypotension) **and** antibiotic initiation all occur. This definition avoids ambiguous borderline cases and aligns with clinical consensus criteria. The prediction window is measured from ICU admission to 90 days post-admission, and cases developing sepsis within the first 24 hours are excluded to ensure a future outcome window.

### 2. Data Splitting: Temporal Stratification

The data is split chronologically, not randomly. Months 1–24 form the training set (~18,000 admissions), months 25–30 form the validation set (~9,000 admissions), and months 31–36 form the held-out test set (~9,000 admissions). This temporal split is essential: random assignment would allow the model to exploit spurious associations between note verbosity, seasonal coding patterns, and outcomes. Temporal splitting ensures that validation and test data truly represent prospective prediction—replicating deployment where the model encounters future admissions it has never seen.

Additionally, any admissions from secondary facilities within the system are excluded from the primary analysis and reserved for a post-hoc robustness check. This stratification rules out site-specific documentation or sepsis management protocols from confounding the results.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Structured features (baseline):** Forty engineered variables derived from vital signs and laboratory values recorded in the first 24 hours: mean, minimum, maximum, and linear-regression trend for systolic/diastolic blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation; identical summary statistics for creatinine, BUN, lactate, platelet count, WBC, hemoglobin, procalcitonin, and bilirubin; binary indicators for vasopressor use, mechanical ventilation initiation, and fluid balance status; and medication class counts. All summaries require a minimum of two timepoints; trends are estimated via least-squares regression.

**Text features (intervention):** Raw clinical progress notes from the first 24 hours, concatenated in chronological order, whitespace-normalized, and tokenized using a frozen pretrained biomedical language model vocabulary (e.g., PubMedBERT). The text is padded or truncated to 512 tokens. No external data or label information is encoded into the text input.

**Preprocessing fit point:** All transformers—scalers (StandardScaler for continuous values), missing-value imputers, text tokenizers, and engineered feature statistics—are fit on the training set only. These fitted transformers are applied to validation and test data using training-set parameters, preventing data leakage and ensuring realistic preprocessing conditions.

### 4. Models and Baseline

Two models are trained:

**Baseline:** Logistic regression or gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost), whichever achieves higher validation performance. Hyperparameters (regularization type and strength for logistic regression; tree depth, learning rate, and number of estimators for XGBoost) are selected via grid search on the validation set. Both models receive identical tuning effort and train only on the 40 structured features.

**Intervention:** A transformer-based architecture with two input streams. Stream A: a BERT-like encoder (4 transformer layers, 8 attention heads, 512 hidden dimensions, pretrained on biomedical text and fine-tuned during training) processes concatenated clinical notes and outputs a [CLS] token representation. Stream B: a small multi-layer perceptron (2 hidden layers with 64 units, ReLU activation) processes scaled structured features. The two streams are concatenated and fed to a final logistic classification head. Dropout (0.2) is applied to hidden layers. Both models use class-weighted binary cross-entropy loss to account for sepsis imbalance (~20% prevalence, verified empirically).

The baseline is justified as a fair control: it uses only historically standard data (vitals and labs), receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning, and is evaluated under identical conditions and label definitions.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Sensitivity (recall) at a fixed specificity of 90%, denoted Sensitivity@Spec90. This metric aligns with the operational goal—early identification of high-risk patients to enable intervention—rather than abstract AUC. A 90% specificity threshold corresponds to approximately 1 false alarm per 10 admissions, a clinically feasible alert burden in ICU workflows. Sensitivity@Spec90 is threshold-dependent and reflects real deployment: a clinician alert system would be configured to this specificity, and success is measured by true-positive capture.

**Secondary metrics:** Area under the ROC curve (AUROC) measures overall discrimination and detects Goodhart's law effects (gaming one threshold). Positive predictive value at 90% specificity (PPV@Spec90) quantifies alert precision and clinician trust. Time-to-detection (median hours from alert to documented sepsis onset among true positives) measures clinical actionability. Brier score and calibration plots assess probability calibration. Sensitivity at 85% and 95% specificity are reported for robustness.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation Strategy

Hyperparameters for both baseline and intervention models are tuned by grid search on the validation set (months 25–30). Each candidate configuration is evaluated on Sensitivity@Spec90 on the validation set. The configuration achieving the highest validation Sensitivity@Spec90 is selected. The decision threshold (the specificity cutoff corresponding to 90% specificity on validation data) is calibrated on the validation set and applied to the test set without adjustment.

The test set is sealed before training and used exactly once at the end. All primary and secondary metrics are computed on the test set and reported with 95% confidence intervals (via stratified bootstrap resampling, 1,000 replicates).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

The known confound—that documentation timing, verbosity, or clinician suspicion may drive apparent gains in a text-based model—is addressed through post-hoc analyses (reported alongside but not replacing primary results): (1) **Ablation:** train the intervention model on increasing amounts of text (first note only, cumulative notes) to detect verbosity artifacts. (2) **Attention analysis:** inspect transformer attention weights and feature importance to identify whether high-weight terms are clinically meaningful or generic documentation patterns. (3) **Site stratification (secondary):** evaluate on held-out secondary facility data to check whether gains generalize or are site-specific. (4) **Temporal sensitivity (secondary):** split test results by early and late months; sustained performance across time argues against documentation-drift artifacts.

This design establishes that notes provide additional predictive signal **at the specified specificity threshold, in the specified population, during the study period**. It does not establish causality, generalization to other institutions, or robustness to major shifts in documentation practices. The scope of claims is bounded by the temporal split and site-specific cohort.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity incident detection, a graph neural network trained on time-windowed endpoint, VPN, and SSO identity events will detect compromised accounts better than a flat tabular gradient-boosted baseline, as measured by AUROC and especially precision at very low false-positive rates on future weeks of data.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise identity and access monitoring from endpoint, VPN, and SSO logs
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Graph Neural Networks for Enterprise Compromise Detection

## Objective
Test whether a temporal graph neural network (GNN) detects compromised accounts with higher precision at low false-positive rates than a gradient-boosted baseline, using production identity and endpoint event data.

## 1. Data and Labels

Collect 18 months of timestamped event logs from endpoint agents, VPN concentrators, and SSO systems. Events include login attempts (successful/failed), geolocation, device identifiers, IP addresses, privilege changes, and session metadata. Derive positive labels (compromised accounts) exclusively from security team dispositions: confirmed incidents from alert triage, post-incident investigations, or threat intelligence correlation. A positive label marks the earliest timestamp in the incident window where abnormal behavior was first evident. Exclude uncertain or partial dispositions from the labeled training and validation sets; hold them in a separate corpus for robustness checking. Negative labels are user-device pairs with 90+ days of continuous clean activity and no analyst flags. This ensures label confidence and prevents noisy pseudo-negatives from corrupting training.

## 2. Temporal Stratified Split with Entity Disjointness

Apply a strict temporal split to prevent future information leakage: allocate months 1–7.2 as training (60%), months 7.2–10.8 as validation (20%), and months 10.8–18 as the held-out test set (20%). Within each split, stratify by positive class to maintain realistic imbalance while ensuring stable metric estimates.

Additionally, construct a secondary entity-disjoint test set: for every user ID and device ID involved in a positive (compromised) case in the test window, remove all prior events involving those same entities from the training set. This ensures the model has never encountered these specific users or devices before test time. If this disjoint set is too small, use a stratified subset. Compare GNN and GBDT performance on both standard and entity-disjoint test sets. A large performance drop on the entity-disjoint set signals the model is memorizing user/device patterns rather than learning attack dynamics.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Construct two parallel feature streams. **Tabular stream**: for each user-device-day window, compute failed login counts, geographic anomalies (IP outside historical range), VPN connection patterns (unusual times, protocol, duration), privilege escalation counts, speed-of-travel violations, session counts, and bytes transferred. Apply log scaling to skewed features, one-hot or target-encode categorical variables (OS, country), and impute missing values with domain defaults (e.g., failed logins → 0). Fit all scalers and encoders on training data only; apply them unchanged to validation and test.

**Graph stream**: construct a dynamic attributed multigraph where nodes are users, devices, and IP addresses. Edges represent within-24-hour interactions (user-device login, device-VPN, VPN-IP). Assign edge attributes: interaction count, outcome (success/failure flag). Extract per-user-device subgraph features: degree centrality, clustering coefficient, k-hop neighborhood flags (e.g., whether 2-hop neighbors were previously flagged), temporal recency.

No feature encodes the label or uses post-incident information. All features are strictly predictive (prior to the window being classified).

## 4. Models and Baseline

**Proposed model**: Temporal GNN (e.g., temporal GAT or temporal GCN) that ingests the attributed dynamic graph at each day window and produces node embeddings for user-device pairs. A task-specific 2-layer MLP head predicts binary compromise probability. Train end-to-end on training data with backpropagation over 7–14 day history windows (tuned on validation).

**Baseline**: Gradient-boosted decision tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) on tabular features only, using identical preprocessing and time windows. Tune both models on validation data with equivalent search budget (e.g., 100 hyperparameter trials each, evaluated on validation PR-AUC and precision at 1% FPR). The baseline ensures that any GNN gain comes from explicit graph structure modeling, not richer feature engineering.

## 5. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary metric**: Precision at 1% False Positive Rate, computed on the test set. This metric directly maps to analyst workflow: at 1% FPR, alerts are rare and high-confidence, reducing SOC alert fatigue. A 90% precision at 1% FPR means analysts investigate ~10 true compromises and 1 false alert per 11 investigations — operationally feasible.

**Secondary metrics**: AUROC (ranking quality), PR-AUC (appropriate for imbalanced data), recall at 1% FPR (how many compromises caught), precision at 0.1% and 5% FPR (sensitivity analysis), and expected calibration error (whether probabilities are well-calibrated for operational thresholding).

## 6. Model Selection and Evaluation Protocol

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on validation data only. The test set is held completely separate, evaluated exactly once at the end. Report metrics with 95% confidence intervals via stratified bootstrap (1,000 resamples) respecting temporal boundaries. Separately evaluate both models on the entity-disjoint test set and report performance gaps as a robustness check.

## 7. Scope and Controls

This design establishes whether the GNN architecture, given access to relational event data and equivalent tuning, achieves higher precision-at-low-FPR than GBDT. It does not fully control for long-term attacker TTP evolution or deployment covariate shift, but the temporal split on future data (months 16–18 unseen during training) provides evidence of near-term robustness. The entity-disjoint evaluation confirms whether gains generalize to unseen actors or reflect memorization.

hypothesis: Re] Reproducibility Study: Comparing Rewinding and Fine-tuning in Neural Network Pruning
task_prompt:
The paper studies how to retrain neural networks after pruning by comparing three post-pruning strategies: conventional fine-tuning, weight rewinding, and a newly proposed method called learning rate rewinding. The setting is magnitude-based pruning applied after standard training. After pruning a trained model to a target sparsity, each method retrains the remaining sparse network under a different recovery procedure, and the paper evaluates which retraining strategy best preserves accuracy.

In fine-tuning, the pruned network continues training from the final learned weights using a standard post-pruning training schedule. In weight rewinding, the network is pruned after training, but the surviving weights are reset to values they had at an earlier training iteration and then retrained from that point onward, following the rewound portion of the original schedule. In learning rate rewinding, the pruned network keeps the final trained weights instead of resetting them, but the optimizer schedule is rewound so that retraining uses the learning rate trajectory corresponding to an earlier point in training. This method is presented as a simple and broadly applicable alternative to weight rewinding.

The empirical methodology consists of training dense models, pruning them by magnitude, and then retraining the resulting sparse subnetworks across a range of sparsity levels. Performance is compared across the three retraining methods by measuring post-pruning test accuracy. The main experiments are conducted primarily on CIFAR-10, with additional evaluations reported in the original work on larger-scale settings such as ImageNet and WMT16, to support the claim that learning rate rewinding is an effective and general retraining strategy after pruning.

hypothesis: Re] Spatial-Adaptive Network for Single Image Denoising
task_prompt:
The paper proposes Spatial-Adaptive Network (SADNet) for single-image denoising, targeting both synthetic and real-noise removal. The claimed model is an encoder-decoder architecture with a U-Net-like design that incorporates residual spatial-adaptive blocks (RSABs) and context blocks to capture multi-scale image information. A central idea is to use deformable convolutions so that the network can adapt to spatially varying structures such as edges and textures, with the goal of handling spatially variant and channel-dependent noise more effectively than conventional CNN denoisers.

Within this architecture, RSABs are used to make the denoising process spatially adaptive, allowing the model to process larger effective regions while preserving image details that are often oversmoothed by standard convolutional networks. Context blocks are included to aggregate multi-scale information across the encoder-decoder pipeline. The overall design is intended to improve restoration quality on both benchmark synthetic denoising tasks and real-image denoising settings.

The paper evaluates SADNet on synthetic and real noise removal benchmarks and reports qualitative and quantitative comparisons against prior methods. Performance is assessed using standard image restoration metrics such as PSNR and SSIM, and the paper claims state-of-the-art or superior denoising performance relative to compared baselines on the considered datasets.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer-based classifier fine-tuned on the full email thread plus sender-recipient graph context will detect business email compromise and credential-phishing attempts better than a text-only classifier trained on the email body and subject line, as measured by precision-recall performance on a future-time holdout.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email defense in a large organization’s inbound mail gateway
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Email Classifier with Graph Context

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of raw inbound email records from the organization's mail gateway, spanning months 1–18. For each email, we retain: body, subject, headers, sender and recipient identities, attachment metadata, and sender reputation signals (computed from historical sending patterns). The conversation thread structure (prior messages in the same conversation thread) is also preserved, but truncated at the send time of each email to exclude future replies that would not have been available at the time of classification.

Labels are obtained from four corroborating sources: (1) security analyst manual review (primary gold standard), (2) user-reported phishing submissions confirmed by the analyst, (3) sandbox malware verdicts, and (4) post-incident response records linking compromised user accounts to specific emails. An email is labeled positive (malicious) if confirmed by analyst review or incident response; ambiguous or single-signal cases are included only with corroborating evidence. We expect extreme class imbalance (0.5–2% malicious emails) reflecting real-world prevalence.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To avoid optimistic bias from attack pattern leakage, we employ a strict chronological split:
- **Training set:** Months 1–12 (12 months of historical email)
- **Validation set:** Months 13–15 (3 months for hyperparameter tuning)
- **Test set:** Months 16–18 (3 months of future-time holdout, never touched until final evaluation)

Justification: Email attack tactics, sender spoofing strategies, and threat landscapes drift over time. A random split would allow models to implicitly learn patterns from future months that have already occurred, inflating measured performance. The temporal split ensures the experiment reflects the true deployment scenario: training on historical data and evaluating on genuinely future, unseen email campaigns. This also captures concept drift—the degradation of model performance as new threats emerge—which random splits obscure.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model (proposed)** receives:
- Full email thread (all prior messages in the conversation, truncated at send time)
- Email body and subject
- Sender and recipient identities (embedded as learned representations)
- Sender reputation score (computed from historical sending patterns, fit on training data only)
- Graph context: sender-recipient relationships, organizational hierarchy, and local neighborhood structure (1–2 hops), derived from communications prior to the email send time

**Baseline model (text-only)** receives:
- Email body and subject only
- No thread history, no sender/recipient identity, no graph context

All tokenization, vocabulary, and preprocessing transformers (including the sender reputation normalizer) are fit exclusively on the training set. The same fitted transformers are applied to validation and test sets without refitting, preventing future-data leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A BERT or DistilBERT transformer encoder fine-tuned on concatenated email thread and structured graph embeddings. Sender and recipient nodes are embedded in a separate space and optionally integrated via graph attention layers. The model outputs binary classification logits converted to probabilities via sigmoid.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression or XGBoost trained on TF-IDF/bag-of-words features from email body and subject. Both models receive identical hyperparameter tuning effort: grid or random search (same computational budget), cross-validation on the training set to select the best configuration.

Justification: The baseline is a principled text-only classifier, providing a clear ablation of architectural depth and contextual features. Equivalent tuning effort ensures the comparison isolates model capacity, not tuning investment.

### 5. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using precision-recall AUC (AUPRC) as the selection metric. Grid or random search is performed; the configuration achieving the highest validation AUPRC is selected. Both models are then retrained on the full training set (months 1–12) using the selected hyperparameters. The test set is held and never used for tuning or selection.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Precision-recall AUC (AUPRC) on the test set. Justification: Class imbalance makes ROC-AUC misleading. PR-AUC is sensitive to performance on the rare positive class and directly reflects the operational trade-off between catching threats (recall/sensitivity) and minimizing false alarms (precision).

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision at fixed recall targets (90%, 95%, 99%)
- Recall at fixed false-positive rates (reflecting deployment constraints)
- Stratified performance by sender type (external, internal, vendor) to detect if improvements generalize
- Temporal degradation: PR-AUC on early test months (16–17) vs. late test months (18) to assess concept drift

### 7. Controls for Confounds

A known confound exists: observed improvements could be driven by access to richer metadata (sender reputation, graph edges) rather than transformer architecture itself.

**Controls:**
1. **Ablation:** Train the transformer on text only (no thread, no graph, no metadata) and compare to the full model. If text-only transformer performs as well as the baseline, the advantage is architectural. If it underperforms, improvements come from metadata.
2. **Baseline enrichment:** Train the baseline on the same rich feature set (sender reputation, graph statistics engineered as numeric features). If performance remains lower than the transformer, the claim is that transformers better utilize context. If performance converges, improvements are purely from feature access.
3. **Stratification checks:** Separately report performance on emails with/without thread history and with/without multi-hop graph connections. If improvements concentrate only where additional context is available, narrower claims can be justified.

All stratified evaluations use the test set. Final results are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap to account for class imbalance and test-set variability.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a model that combines email text embeddings with sender-reputation and attachment-derived features will detect spear-phishing emails better than a text-only transformer baseline, as measured by higher precision at the same recall on a temporally held-out test set.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise inbound email security for phishing/spear-phishing detection at a large organization or security vendor
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal Phishing Detection with Metadata Enhancement

To test whether multimodal features (text embeddings + sender reputation + attachment indicators) outperform text-only transformers for spear-phishing detection, the following experiment is proposed.

### 1. Data and Labeling

We will use 18 months of production inbound email logs from a large enterprise or security vendor (Jan 2022–Jun 2023), containing 2–5 million messages. The ground-truth label is binary: 1 = confirmed malicious (verified by analyst, sandbox verdict, or user report), 0 = legitimate or unconfirmed. Labels are obtained from security team verdicts recorded at least 30 days post-arrival to allow confirmatory analysis; emails lacking high-confidence labels are excluded to prevent noisy training data. The dataset reflects realistic class imbalance (~0.5–2% positive) and attack drift (tactics evolve over time).

### 2. Temporal Train–Validation–Test Split

We employ a chronological split (no random shuffling):
- **Train:** Jan–Sep 2022 (9 months, ~50% of data)
- **Validation:** Oct–Dec 2022 (3 months, ~17%)
- **Test:** Jan–Jun 2023 (6 months, ~33%, held until final evaluation)

Temporal splitting is essential because: (1) phishing tactics and malicious infrastructure evolve; random mixing would allow models to memorize known bad domains appearing in both sets, inflating generalization; (2) production deployment requires performance on future emails; (3) class imbalance and attack patterns shift seasonally. The test window represents a realistic deployment scenario unseen during tuning.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing and feature transformers are fit on the training set only and frozen before validation. Features are structured in three groups, all available at email arrival time (before analyst verdict):

- **Text embeddings (768–1024 dim):** Pre-trained transformer (e.g., DistilBERT) applied to email body and subject; no task-specific fine-tuning to isolate semantic signal.
- **Sender reputation:** External signals only—domain whitelist/blacklist status, SPF/DKIM/DMARC compliance, domain age in days (binned), historical complaint count (capped at 100). Derived from reputation feeds at arrival; not updated post-delivery.
- **Attachment features:** Count, file types (one-hot encoded), total size (log-scaled, binned), presence of macro-enabled documents.

Notably excluded: post-delivery sandbox verdicts, user quarantine actions, or organization-specific internal policies—these are unavailable at inference time and would confound the evaluation.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Primary model:** Gradient boosted tree ensemble (LightGBM/XGBoost) trained on concatenated text embeddings + metadata (~900–1000 input features). Trees capture non-linear interactions and handle mixed feature types without additional encoding.

**Baseline:** Pre-trained transformer fine-tuned on text only (email body + subject), hyperparameter-tuned on validation data with equivalent effort. The baseline answers: 'Does metadata improve text-only performance?' Both models receive the same tuning budget and validation data.

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set using the primary metric (precision at 95% recall).

### 5. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary:** Precision at 95% recall on test set, with 95% CI (bootstrap stratified by month). Justification: security teams must catch nearly all phishing (recall is a hard constraint); the question is whether the combined model achieves higher precision (fewer false alerts) under that constraint.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall at fixed false-positive rate (e.g., 0.5% FPR)
- AUC-PR for the precision-recall curve
- Calibration (ECE, Brier score)
- Stratified precision at 95% recall by: sender novelty (novel domains unseen in training), attachment presence, temporal month (to detect drift)

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Validation data (Oct–Dec 2022) is used for threshold tuning and hyperparameter search; no model selection occurs on the test set. The test set is locked and evaluated once at the end, reporting performance with confidence intervals and permutation test p-values.

### 7. Confound Controls

- **Domain memorization check:** Test performance is stratified by sender-domain novelty (first appearance in test window vs. training). Sharp performance degradation on novel domains would indicate the model memorizes reputation lists rather than learning semantic patterns.
- **Reputation leakage audit:** A security engineer reviews feature code to verify no post-delivery signals (internal quarantine rules, user flags, organization-specific blocklists) are included.
- **Label quality:** Verdicts ≥30 days old to allow confirmatory evidence; rapid-verdict or automated-only emails excluded.
- **Class imbalance:** Class weights applied during training (or cost-sensitive loss) to prevent minority-class neglect.
- **Statistical significance:** Permutation test comparing primary model and baseline precision differences (p-value reported).
- **Scope:** Findings apply to this organization and window; generalization requires external evaluation or multi-site holdout.

hypothesis: For telecom customer support, a transformer-based classifier that uses the full sequence of chat messages and call-center metadata will predict near-term customer churn more accurately than a baseline model using only aggregated tabular interaction features, as measured on a temporally held-out month of customers.
domain: Telecommunications customer support and churn prediction for a subscription service
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Churn Prediction vs. Aggregated Baseline

### 1. Data and Label Definition

This experiment uses 18 months of historical customer data spanning customer accounts, support chat transcripts with full timestamps, call-center logs, and churn outcomes from a telecommunications provider. Churn is defined as subscription non-renewal or cancellation within 30 days following a prediction cutoff date. Customers with incomplete records or service suspensions are excluded. The raw data covers the full customer population during this period with no sampling.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To prevent leakage and respect the causal structure of churn prediction, data are divided chronologically: months 1–12 form the training set, months 13–15 the validation set, and months 16–18 the held-out test set. This split reflects real deployment: the model must predict churn for customers in month 16 using only information available through month 15. Temporal splits are essential because churn is subject to seasonality, promotional campaigns, and regulatory changes; random splitting would allow the model to exploit future patterns and produce inflated performance estimates.

### 3. Feature Construction

Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Transformer input:** The full chronological sequence of support chat messages for each customer, concatenated with call-center metadata (agent experience, issue category, resolution time, repeat-contact indicator). Text is tokenized using a vocabulary learned from the training set; vocabulary size and out-of-vocabulary handling are fixed before validation and test are processed.

**Baseline input:** Aggregated tabular features computed from the same events: message count (total and in the last 30 days), average message length, mode issue category, call duration summary statistics, account tenure, plan type, and billing events in the prior 90 days. All features use only information strictly before the prediction cutoff; no future interactions or billing are included.

All preprocessing (tokenization, scaling, encoding) is fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test, preventing information leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** A BERT-style sequence encoder is fine-tuned on the support chat sequences with concatenated metadata. A classification head outputs a churn probability via sigmoid activation. Training runs for up to 10 epochs with early stopping on validation loss, selecting the best checkpoint on the validation set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the aggregated tabular features alone. This baseline is given equivalent tuning rigor: regularization strength (L2 penalty) is tuned via grid search over [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0], evaluated on the validation set. Logistic regression is the standard production baseline in churn modeling and provides a fair, interpretable comparison that isolates the value of sequence modeling.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Under class imbalance and constrained outreach budgets, AUPRC directly reflects business value: it summarizes the true-positive rate achieved as false positives accumulate across the ranking. The top-ranked customers by predicted probability are targeted for retention contact; AUPRC measures how effectively the model concentrates churners at the top.

**Secondary metrics:** 
- Precision at top K (5% and 10% of customers): the false-positive rate experienced at fixed budget.
- Recall at fixed precision: how many true churners are captured while maintaining acceptable false-positive rate.
- Calibration (Brier score, calibration plots): alignment between predicted probabilities and observed churn rates, critical for downstream threshold tuning.
- ROC-AUC: technical performance across all thresholds.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

All hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 13–15). Transformer hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, dropout, early stopping patience) are tuned via random search over a predefined space, evaluated on validation loss. The baseline's regularization is tuned on the same validation set. The test set is not touched during development.

### 7. Confound Controls

To ensure the gain is not driven by length exploitation or recency bias, a secondary baseline is trained on aggregated features *plus* explicit length and recency features (token count, message count, time since last contact). If the transformer outperforms this length-controlled baseline, the win reflects true content understanding, not artifact.

Test results are stratified by customer segment (plan, tenure, region) to detect systematic performance disparities. Customers who received post-cutoff retention interventions are flagged and results reported with and without them, confirming the model predicts churn propensity rather than intervention response.

The 1-month gap between validation (month 15) and test start (month 16) allows seasonal and campaign dynamics to shift, reducing overfitting to a specific period.

### 8. Final Evaluation

Once training and validation conclude, the selected transformer model and tuned baseline are evaluated exactly once on the held-out test set (months 16–18). Primary and secondary metrics are computed and reported without further tuning or threshold adjustment on test data. The result establishes whether sequential transformer modeling on support transcripts provides meaningful churn prediction improvement over a strong aggregated baseline under realistic, temporally forward-looking conditions.

hypothesis: Re] Satellite Image Time Series Classification with Pixel-Set Encoders and Temporal Self-Attention
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a method for crop type classification from multispectral satellite image time series by combining spatial encoding of image content with temporal modeling across observations. The input consists of sequences of medium-resolution optical satellite images acquired over time for agricultural parcels. Rather than applying a conventional convolutional network to each image, the method introduces a pixel-set encoder that represents each observation from a set of sampled pixels and associated descriptive statistics, producing a compact embedding for each date.

These per-timestamp embeddings are then processed with a temporal attention encoder, an adaptation of the Transformer architecture designed for satellite image time series. The temporal module uses self-attention to model dependencies across the sequence and to aggregate information from multiple acquisition dates, with the goal of capturing phenological patterns relevant to crop discrimination. The overall architecture is intended to jointly exploit spatial variability within parcel imagery and temporal evolution across the growing season.

The claimed evaluation studies both the proposed architecture and its classification performance on crop type recognition from satellite time series data. The paper positions the pixel-set encoder as an alternative to CNN-based image encoders for medium-resolution remote sensing inputs, and the temporal self-attention module as a sequence model tailored to Earth observation time series. Performance is assessed by training the full model for supervised crop classification and comparing its results to relevant alternatives on the target dataset.

hypothesis: For emergency department triage, a gradient-boosted model using only structured triage variables and prior utilization will be outperformed by a multimodal model that adds free-text triage notes and chief-complaint embeddings when predicting 24-hour hospital admission, measured on a future-time holdout.
domain: Emergency department operations using triage records, free-text chief complaints, and prior encounter history from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experimental Design: Multimodal vs. Structured Triage Models for 24-Hour ED Admission Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling

We will use 36 months of consecutive ED visits from a multi-hospital health system, sourced from the ED information system and EHR. The dataset will include all visits with complete structured triage data (vitals, acuity scores, demographics, comorbidities, prior encounters) and free-text triage notes written by nursing staff at initial assessment. We will exclude visits with missing critical vitals, transfers from other acute facilities, or incomplete disposition documentation.

The label is defined as hospital admission (yes/no) recorded in the disposition coding within 24 hours of ED arrival. Observation stays are excluded. Critically, the label is sourced from clinical disposition records independent of the narrative triage note content, preventing the note text from appearing in the label definition.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

We will use a chronological split to respect the temporal and operational non-stationarity of ED data: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 for the held-out test set. Within each temporal segment, we will stratify by both hospital site and admission outcome. This two-level stratification ensures that each site is represented proportionally (controlling for site-specific documentation and admission thresholds) and that admission rates are balanced across splits (preserving label distribution and enabling fair metric comparison).

The temporal approach prevents leakage from future triage practice shifts or patient acuity trends and aligns the evaluation with the deployment scenario, where the model will be applied to future ED visits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured baseline features include: age, sex, vital signs (HR, BP, RR, O2 sat, temperature), ESI acuity level, arrival mode, count of prior ED visits in the past 12 months, prior admission history (binary), ICD-10 comorbidity flags (top 20 by frequency), and site identifier.

Multimodal augmented features add: (1) free-text triage note embeddings generated by a pre-trained, frozen biomedical language model (e.g., BioBERT or Clinical-BERT trained on external corpora); (2) chief-complaint embeddings (if available separately); (3) note length in tokens.

All preprocessing transformers (numeric scaling, categorical encoding, imputation, text tokenization, and embedding models) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents the model from learning validation or test data statistics. Pre-trained text embedding models are not fine-tuned on the ED dataset; they remain frozen to avoid overfitting to this domain.

### 4. Model and Baseline

Both the baseline and multimodal models are gradient-boosted tree ensembles (XGBoost or LightGBM). Hyperparameters (max depth, learning rate, number of estimators, L2 regularization) are tuned using grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set with identical search budgets for both models. The only difference between the two is the feature set: the baseline uses structured features only; the multimodal model adds text embeddings and note metadata.

This design ensures a fair comparison—any performance difference is attributable to the text information, not to differences in model class, tuning effort, or data access.

### 5. Model Selection

Hyperparameters are selected via 5-fold cross-validation within the validation set (months 25–30), evaluated on the primary metric (AUROC). The configuration yielding the highest mean AUROC is locked. The test set is held out throughout development and is evaluated only once, after final model selection, to report definitive hypothesis results.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUROC on the test set. AUROC measures discrimination across all thresholds, is robust to class imbalance, and aligns with the need to identify high-risk admissions for bed planning and triage escalation without specifying the operating point a priori.

Secondary metrics include AUPRC (for rare-outcome sensitivity), sensitivity at fixed specificity levels (operationally relevant for workflow escalation), expected calibration error (essential for confidence-based staffing decisions), and PPV/NPV at clinically relevant sensitivity thresholds.

### 7. Confound Controls

Site-level stratification in the split controls for site-specific triage practices and admission thresholds. Triage notes are validated to originate at check-in, before disposition decisions, preventing post-hoc leakage. Note length is included as an explicit feature in both models, allowing the model to account for verbosity. A secondary analysis evaluates performance on standardized or minimal-text subsets to confirm that gains are not driven by documentation volume alone.

The hypothesis is narrowly scoped: we test whether the multimodal model exhibits superior discrimination of admission risk on a held-out future cohort. The experiment does not establish clinical efficacy or causation, only comparative predictive accuracy.
